proposals.title: Co-occurrence and Common Etiology: A Latent Class Analysis of Child Maltreatment and Victimization Types in the Australian Child Maltreatment Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's own work, often treats distinct victimization categories (e.g., physical abuse, sexual abuse, witnessing violence) as independent outcomes for prevalence and correlation studies. This siloed approach may obscure a fundamental reality: these experiences frequently co-occur within the same child, suggesting they are interdependent symptoms of a single underlying family or community risk context. Understanding this shared etiology is critical for developing more holistic, family-centered prevention and intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific types of child victimization (physical abuse, sexual abuse, emotional abuse, neglect, exposure to domestic violence) are not independent events but are manifestations of a smaller number of latent family risk profiles. These latent profiles will be more strongly associated with child emotional outcomes than any single victimization type.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) dataset. Using latent class analysis (LCA), we will identify distinct, empirically derived subgroups of children based on their patterns of exposure across the five measured maltreatment types. The optimal number of classes will be determined using fit indices (AIC, BIC, entropy). We will then use regression analyses to test how these latent class memberships, compared to individual maltreatment types, predict key emotional outcomes (e.g., depression, anxiety symptoms) available in the ACMS, controlling for demographic covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field beyond counting discrete victimization types by modeling their natural co-occurrence patterns. It will provide empirical evidence for a more parsimonious, family-risk-profile model of victimization. The findings will directly inform the design of integrated screening tools and multi-targeted interventions that address the core family dysfunction rather than isolated maltreatment types.
proposals.novelty: While co-occurrence is acknowledged, this study formally tests the hypothesis that standard categories are non-independent symptoms of latent family risk, using a robust national dataset and a person-centered analytic technique (LCA) not typically applied to the full spectrum of ACMS measures to derive etiological profiles.

proposals.title: Identifying Universal Protective Environments: A Secondary Analysis of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) Focusing on Non-Victimized Children
proposals.motivation: Child maltreatment research has predominantly focused on risk factors and victimization outcomes. The recent Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) revealed that 29% of Australians aged 16-65+ reported no childhood maltreatment. This significant subgroup represents a critical, understudied opportunity to identify robust, universal protective environments that may operate across multiple forms of maltreatment, moving beyond individual resilience to understand systemic and familial buffers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that non-victimized children, as identified in the ACMS, will share a distinct constellation of protective environmental factors—spanning family, community, and socio-economic domains—that are robustly associated with the absence of all measured maltreatment types, even when controlling for known demographic risk factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of the ACMS dataset. Using the detailed items on childhood experiences, family characteristics, and community context, we will first define the 'non-victimized' group (reporting no exposure to physical, sexual, emotional abuse, neglect, or exposure to domestic violence). We will then employ multivariate logistic regression to identify factors uniquely associated with non-victimization. To model complex interactions among protective factors, we will use machine learning techniques, specifically a random forest classifier, to identify the most predictive combinations of environmental and familial items for membership in the non-victimized group.
proposals.contribution: This study will shift the field's focus from deficit to strength-based models by empirically identifying the environmental correlates of safety. It will provide concrete, evidence-based targets for universal prevention programs aimed at creating protective environments for all children, complementing existing risk-focused interventions.
proposals.novelty: While resilience research exists, this study uniquely leverages a large, national dataset to systematically invert the analytical lens from 'what went wrong' to 'what went right' for a substantial population subgroup, seeking universal protective environments rather than individual traits or maltreatment-specific buffers.

proposals.title: Estimating the Latent Prevalence of Child Maltreatment: A Measurement Error Modeling Approach Using the Australian Child Maltreatment Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, landmark surveys like the ACMS provide crucial prevalence estimates for child maltreatment, but these are acknowledged to be lower-bound figures due to under-reporting influenced by stigma, memory biases, and definitional ambiguity. This systematic measurement error distorts our understanding of the true scale of victimization and its associated risk factors, potentially leading to under-resourced prevention and intervention efforts. Correcting for this bias is essential for accurate epidemiological modeling and policy planning.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying measurement error models to ACMS data will yield a latent prevalence distribution for child maltreatment types (physical, sexual, emotional abuse, neglect, exposure to domestic violence) that is significantly higher than the directly reported rates, and this corrected distribution will show stronger and more precise associations with known correlates like mental health outcomes.
proposals.method: We will treat ACMS self-report items as imperfect indicators of a latent, true victimization status. Using a Bayesian framework, we will implement a latent class analysis (LCA) model, potentially extended to a Bayesian structural equation model (BSEM) with a latent response variable. The model will incorporate parameters for sensitivity (true positive rate) and specificity (true negative rate) of survey items, informed by prior distributions derived from pre-2026 validation studies (e.g., test-retest reliability, multi-method studies). We will analyze data for each maltreatment type separately and jointly, examining model fit using posterior predictive checks and WAIC.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first nationally representative, bias-corrected estimates of child maltreatment prevalence for Australia, moving beyond simple lower bounds. It will offer a methodological template for re-analyzing other major survey datasets (e.g., the US National Survey of Children's Exposure to Violence) to produce more accurate population burden estimates and refined models of associated emotional and behavioral outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While under-reporting is widely acknowledged, most pre-2026 research treats it as a limitation rather than a quantifiable parameter to be modeled. This proposal innovatively applies formal measurement error correction techniques, common in other fields, to child victimization survey data to statistically estimate the latent prevalence, moving from qualitative acknowledgment to quantitative adjustment.

proposals.title: Developing a Child-Reported Severity-Weighted Burden Score: Refining the Measurement of Polyvictimization
proposals.motivation: Current measures of child polyvictimization, including the Juvenile Victimization Questionnaire (JVQ), typically sum victimization types as equally impactful counts. This obscures the true burden of maltreatment, as a child experiencing multiple low-impact events may receive the same score as a child experiencing one highly severe event. Grounded in Finkelhor's pre-2026 work on the differential impacts of abuse types and the methodology of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), this research addresses a critical measurement gap to better predict mental health and functional outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: A polyvictimization burden score, weighted by child-reported severity ratings for specific victimization items, will demonstrate stronger associations with adverse emotional and behavioral outcomes than a simple, unweighted count of victimization types.
proposals.method: We will utilize existing data from the ACMS, which includes detailed child maltreatment items and child-reported emotional impact. For each endorsed JVQ screener item (e.g., sexual abuse, physical assault, cyberstalking), we will link it to its associated follow-up question on perceived severity or upset. Using factor analysis and item response theory (IRT) models, we will derive empirical severity weights for each victimization type. These weights will be used to create a continuous burden score (sum of weights), which will then be compared against the traditional count score in regression models predicting outcomes like PTSD, depression, and anxiety.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, empirically grounded method for creating a severity-weighted burden of victimization score. It will advance the field beyond simple counts, offering clinicians and researchers a more precise tool for risk assessment and resource allocation. The findings will directly inform the refinement of instruments like the JVQ for future population surveys and etiological research.
proposals.novelty: While prior research acknowledges severity differences, this study systematically quantifies child-reported severity weights across the full spectrum of victimization types to create a composite burden metric, moving beyond expert-derived or offense-based legal severity classifications.

proposals.title: Mapping Localized Risk: A Small-Area Estimation of Child Maltreatment Prevalence Using Hierarchical Bayesian Models and National Survey Data
proposals.motivation: National surveys like the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) reveal population-level prevalence of child maltreatment, but resource allocation and targeted prevention require local estimates. Pre-2026, public health and crime prevention strategies are often hampered by the lack of reliable, granular data on victimization risk at the county or neighborhood level. Small-area estimation methods from demography can bridge this gap, transforming national survey data into actionable local intelligence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hierarchical Bayesian models, incorporating both individual-level survey responses and area-level demographic covariates from census data, can produce statistically reliable and valid estimates of child maltreatment prevalence for small geographic areas where direct survey estimates are too imprecise.
proposals.method: We will apply hierarchical Bayesian models, specifically Bayesian multilevel regression with post-stratification (MRP). Using ACMS individual-level data on maltreatment types (emotional, physical, sexual abuse, neglect, exposure to domestic violence) and associated emotional/mental health items, we will build multilevel logistic regression models. These models will incorporate random effects for geographic areas and fixed effects for demographic covariates (e.g., age, sex, socioeconomic status from census data). The models will then be post-stratified against the full demographic composition of each target small area (e.g., Australian Statistical Area Level 2) to generate posterior prevalence estimates with credible intervals.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first fine-grained, model-based maps of child maltreatment risk across Australia, directly informing localized public health and child protection interventions. It will advance methodological integration between victimization research and spatial epidemiology, providing a replicable framework for other nations with national survey data. The findings will clarify how area-level characteristics are associated with maltreatment risk, beyond individual-level factors.
proposals.novelty: While small-area estimation is established in public health for diseases, its application to complex, stigmatized experiences like child maltreatment—particularly using the rich, multi-type data from the ACMS—is novel. This moves beyond descriptive national reporting to predictive, localized risk modeling tailored for prevention planning.

proposals.title: Identifying Protective Ecologies: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Communities and Families with Zero Child Victimization in the Australian Child Maltreatment Study
proposals.motivation: Decades of research, including Finkelhor's foundational work, have focused on risk factors for child maltreatment and victimization. While crucial, this deficit model offers limited guidance for primary prevention. Identifying and understanding settings where victimization is absent, despite the presence of known risk factors, can reveal actionable protective community and family dynamics. This shifts the focus from pathology to positive deviance, offering a new blueprint for prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, identifiable combinations of community-level social cohesion, institutional safeguards, and family-level emotional climate and communication practices will be significantly associated with a 'zero victimization' outcome across multiple maltreatment types (physical, sexual, emotional abuse, neglect, exposure to domestic violence).
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) dataset, applying a case-control design. Cases ('zero victimization') will be participants reporting no lifetime experiences of any measured maltreatment. Controls will be a matched sample reporting victimization. We will use logistic regression and Classification and Regression Tree (CART) analysis to identify the constellation of protective factors (e.g., neighborhood trust, school safety policies, parental warmth, sibling relationship quality) most predictive of the zero-victimization outcome. Qualitative follow-up interviews will be conducted with a subsample from identified 'protective' clusters to elucidate mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond cataloging risk to model the specific ecological configurations that prevent victimization. It will provide an empirical basis for strength-based prevention programs and policies. The findings will also refine developmental victimology theory by specifying how protective processes operate across different levels of a child's ecology.
proposals.novelty: While resilience research exists, systematically analyzing 'zero victimization' as a distinct outcome in a nationally representative dataset is novel. It applies a public health 'positive deviance' framework directly to the field of child victimization, seeking to learn from success rather than failure.

proposals.title: Beyond Exploitation: Identifying Contextual and Emotional Factors that Mitigate Harm in Age-Discrepant Relationships Involving Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Prevailing models of child sexual abuse, including Finkelhor's own preconditions model, often treat significant age differences as a primary risk factor for exploitation and trauma. However, this monolithic view may obscure a more nuanced reality, potentially pathologizing non-exploitative relationships and limiting effective prevention by failing to distinguish harmful from benign contexts. This investigation is crucial for refining theoretical models and informing more precise legal and clinical responses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific contextual factors (e.g., perceived voluntariness, absence of coercion, emotional reciprocity) and positive emotional appraisals by the younger participant are associated with self-reported non-traumatic outcomes in age-discrepant relationships, even when such relationships meet statutory definitions of abuse.
proposals.method: Leveraging the methodological framework of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), we will design a supplemental module for a national survey. This module will use detailed behavioral items to identify adolescents and young adults reporting age-discrepant sexual contact before age 18. We will employ validated scales to assess associated emotional experiences (e.g., feelings of agency, regret, positive affect) and perceived coercion. Multivariate logistic regression will model the relationship between these contextual/emotional factors and trauma outcomes, controlling for demographic variables and other maltreatment types.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, population-based empirical test of whether and how context moderates harm in age-discrepant relationships. It will contribute a more differentiated evidence base for policy debates on statutory rape and consent laws. The findings will also advance developmental victimology by clarifying the constructs of exploitation and harm, moving beyond age difference as a sole proxy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents the harms of abuse, this study systematically investigates the counterfactual—contexts where harm is absent despite age disparity—a question largely unaddressed in quantitative, population-based research pre-2026. It applies Finkelhol's focus on clarifying constructs (e.g., sibling aggression vs. abuse) to the complex domain of age-discrepant relationships.

proposals.title: A Longitudinal Cohort Study of Child Maltreatment Typologies, Emotional Sequelae, and Long-Term Mental Health Outcomes: Building on the Australian Child Maltreatment Study
proposals.motivation: Despite robust cross-sectional evidence linking child maltreatment to adverse outcomes, the long-term trajectories of mental health following specific types and combinations of abuse (e.g., sexual, physical, emotional, neglect, exposure to domestic violence) remain inadequately mapped. The recent Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) provides a critical national prevalence baseline but is not longitudinal. Applying rigorous epidemiological cohort methods is essential to establish temporal precedence, identify sensitive developmental periods, and disentangle the cumulative and unique effects of different maltreatment experiences on emotional and psychiatric outcomes over time.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific typologies and developmental timing of child maltreatment exposure (e.g., early-onset poly-victimization) will predict distinct longitudinal trajectories of emotional dysregulation and subsequent diagnosable mental health disorders in adolescence and young adulthood, with effects mediated by factors like perceived social support and maladaptive coping.
proposals.method: This study will establish a prospective cohort by recruiting a sub-sample of ACMS participants (aged 16-24 at baseline) for follow-up, supplemented by a new younger cohort (aged 10-12). We will employ a multi-wave, accelerated longitudinal design with assessments every 2-3 years. The method centers on applying survival analysis (Cox proportional hazards models) to model time-to-onset of mental health outcomes, and latent growth curve modeling (LGCM) to map trajectories of emotional distress items (e.g., anxiety, depression symptoms). Key independent variables will be the detailed, behaviorally-specific maltreatment items from the ACMS, analyzed for type, severity, chronicity, and developmental period.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal evidence from Australia on how different maltreatment experiences shape mental health across key developmental transitions. It will move beyond association to better inform causal models, identify critical intervention points, and validate the predictive validity of the ACMS measurement items. Findings will directly inform targeted prevention and mental health service planning for victimized youth.
proposals.novelty: While longitudinal studies exist, this proposal uniquely integrates the comprehensive, nationally-representative maltreatment typology data from the ACMS with rigorous epidemiological cohort and trajectory modeling methods to dissect the long-term emotional consequences of specific abuse profiles, a nuanced analysis largely absent in pre-2026 Australian research.

proposals.title: Modeling the Distinctive Pathways of Child Maltreatment Trauma: An Adaptation of Disaster and Combat Stress Frameworks
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 trauma research, particularly from disaster and combat psychology, has developed sophisticated models for understanding stressor characteristics and symptom trajectories. However, these frameworks have not been systematically applied to child maltreatment, which involves distinct interpersonal dynamics, developmental timing, and relational contexts. The Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) provides a unique, large-scale dataset to test whether these established trauma models can elucidate the specific emotional and psychological pathways associated with different types of child abuse and neglect.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific dimensions of traumatic stress identified in disaster/combat models (e.g., perceived life threat, betrayal, moral injury) will differentially predict abuse-specific emotional and mental health outcomes, and that these pathways will vary significantly by maltreatment type (e.g., sexual abuse, physical abuse, emotional abuse, neglect, exposure to domestic violence).
proposals.method: We will conduct secondary analysis of the ACMS data. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will adapt established disaster/combat trauma frameworks (e.g., the dose-response model, the stressor appraisal model) to create latent constructs relevant to child maltreatment (e.g., betrayal trauma, developmental disruption). These constructs will be operationalized using specific ACMS survey items about the abuse characteristics (e.g., frequency, perpetrator relationship, emotional impact) and tested as mediators between the maltreatment experiences and associated mental health outcomes (e.g., PTSD, depression, anxiety).
proposals.contribution: This study will generate a more nuanced, empirically grounded model for how different forms of child maltreatment lead to specific traumatic sequelae, moving beyond generic 'adverse childhood experience' scores. It will provide a testable theoretical bridge between general trauma psychology and the specific field of child victimology, offering clinicians and researchers a refined toolkit for predicting outcomes and tailoring interventions based on abuse-specific trauma pathways.
proposals.novelty: While trauma theory is widely referenced in child maltreatment research, this study is novel in its direct, quantitative adaptation of formal models from disaster/combat psychology to model interpersonal victimization, and in its use of a comprehensive, nationally representative child maltreatment dataset (the ACMS) to test these adapted frameworks.

proposals.title: Mapping the Lifespan Trajectory of Maltreatment Impact: A Longitudinal Analysis of Symptom Evolution from Childhood to Late Adulthood
proposals.motivation: While the prevalence and immediate correlates of child maltreatment are well-documented, a critical gap exists in understanding how its traumatic impact dynamically evolves across the entire lifespan. Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's work on polyvictimization and the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), has established robust cross-sectional links but lacks longitudinal data to trace how specific abuse types (e.g., sexual, sibling aggression) manifest in emotional and behavioral items at different life stages. This limits the development of age-targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: The symptomatic and functional impact of child maltreatment follows distinct, non-linear trajectories across the lifespan, with specific abuse types (e.g., sexual, emotional) predicting unique patterns of symptom evolution (e.g., internalizing in adolescence, somatic in mid-adulthood) that are moderated by revictimization and social support.
proposals.method: We propose a secondary longitudinal analysis of existing cohort studies (e.g., linking ACMS methodology to longitudinal panels) to track individuals from childhood into late adulthood. Using latent growth curve modeling (LGCM) and group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM), we will map developmental pathways of outcomes measured by established emotional and behavioral items (e.g., PTSD, depression, relationship functioning). Analyses will test for differential trajectories by maltreatment type, severity, and co-occurrence, controlling for associated factors like socioeconomic status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, lifespan map of maltreatment impact trajectories, identifying critical transition points for intervention. It will advance the field beyond static risk associations by clarifying how and when specific harms manifest, directly informing the timing and focus of clinical and policy responses. The work will also extend developmental victimology by applying Finkelhor's conceptual frameworks to longitudinal data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in its lifespan perspective—from childhood to late adulthood—and its application of advanced longitudinal modeling to differentiate evolving symptom pathways by maltreatment type, moving beyond cross-sectional correlates to dynamic processes of adaptation and deterioration.

proposals.title: Mapping the Pathways: A Causal Mediation Analysis of Abuse Characteristics and Specific Symptom Profiles in Child Sexual Abuse
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's own work and the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), robustly documents that child sexual abuse (CSA) leads to diverse mental health outcomes. However, the field lacks a detailed understanding of *why* specific abuse characteristics (e.g., penetration, use of force, perpetrator relationship) are associated with distinct symptom profiles (e.g., PTSD, depression, dissociation). Clarifying these causal pathways is critical for developing targeted, mechanism-informed interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between specific abuse characteristics (e.g., penetration, force) and distinct symptom profiles is significantly mediated by specific post-abuse cognitive and emotional processes, such as traumatic sexualization, betrayal, and powerlessness, as outlined in Finkelhor and Browne's (1985) traumagenic dynamics model.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of the ACMS dataset, focusing on participants reporting CSA. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will test a series of causal mediation models. Abuse characteristics (predictors) will be operationalized using ACMS items (e.g., type of act, use of force). Mediators will be measured via validated scales for traumagenic dynamics (e.g., items on shame, betrayal, power). Outcomes will be specific symptom profiles (PTSD, depression, anxiety) from the study's mental health assessment. Path analysis will quantify direct and indirect effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond correlational findings to identify the specific mechanisms through which abuse features translate into psychological harm. It will empirically test and potentially refine the traumagenic dynamics model. The results will provide an evidence base for clinicians to tailor therapeutic approaches (e.g., targeting betrayal cognitions vs. powerlessness) based on a survivor's specific abuse experience.
proposals.novelty: While the traumagenic model is influential, it has rarely been tested as a set of formal, statistical mediators linking granular abuse characteristics to differentiated symptom outcomes in a large, nationally representative sample like the ACMS.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Modular Intervention for Child Sexual Abuse: Testing the Efficacy of Tailoring to Perpetrator Relationship and Maternal Support
proposals.motivation: Child sexual abuse (CSA) is a prevalent and damaging form of maltreatment, yet intervention outcomes are highly variable. Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's work on abuse types and the Australian Child Maltreatment Study, indicates that abuse characteristics (e.g., intra-familial vs. extra-familial perpetrator) and post-disclosure maternal support are critical moderators of child trauma and recovery. However, most evidence-based therapies are standardized and do not systematically tailor treatment to these key contextual factors, potentially limiting their effectiveness for significant subsets of victims.
proposals.hypothesis: A modular, trauma-focused cognitive-behavioral therapy (TF-CBT) protocol that explicitly tailors treatment components to the child's relationship to the perpetrator (intra-familial vs. extra-familial) and the level of maternal belief and support will yield significantly greater reductions in child PTSD and depressive symptoms at post-treatment and 6-month follow-up compared to standard, non-tailored TF-CBT.
proposals.method: The study will be a two-arm randomized controlled trial with 300 child-caregiver dyads (children aged 7-12). The tailored intervention arm will use a decision-tree algorithm based on pre-treatment assessment of perpetrator relationship (using items from the Juvenile Victimization Questionnaire) and maternal support (using the Maternal Self-Report Support Questionnaire). This algorithm will prescribe specific treatment modules (e.g., enhanced safety planning for intra-familial cases; dyadic caregiver-child sessions focused on belief and alliance for low-support cases) within a core TF-CBT framework. The control arm will receive manualized TF-CBT. Primary outcomes are child-reported PTSD symptoms (UCLA PTSD Reaction Index) and depressive symptoms. Multilevel modeling will test for interaction effects between treatment condition and the tailoring variables.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first rigorous experimental test of whether tailoring evidence-based therapy to specific abuse characteristics and family context improves efficacy. It will advance the field beyond a 'one-size-fits-all' approach by generating an empirically supported clinical decision protocol. Findings will directly inform more precise and potentially more effective service delivery for a heterogeneous population of CSA victims.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of perpetrator relationship and maternal support is known, their systematic use as pre-treatment tailoring variables in a modular intervention protocol is novel. This moves beyond correlational findings to experimentally test a personalized treatment model, integrating Finkelhor's focus on abuse types with clinical intervention science in a new way.

proposals.title: Profiles of Positive Adaptation: Identifying Protective Factors and Pathways to High Functioning Among Severely Maltreated Children
proposals.motivation: While extensive research documents the deleterious outcomes of child maltreatment, a significant minority of severely abused children exhibit remarkable resilience and superior functioning. Understanding the factors that enable this positive adaptation is critical for pre-2026 child protection and clinical practice, as it can inform strengths-based interventions. This study directly addresses a gap in David Finkelhor's work on maltreatment types and correlates by systematically investigating the inverse of expected negative outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Severely maltreated children who demonstrate high functioning will exhibit distinct, measurable profiles of individual, relational, and community-level protective factors that differ significantly from those of their lower-functioning maltreated peers and non-maltreated high-functioning children.
proposals.method: Leveraging the methodology and infrastructure of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), we will conduct a secondary analysis to identify a sub-sample of children meeting severe abuse criteria yet scoring in the top quartile on validated measures of emotional, social, and academic functioning. Using latent class analysis, we will identify distinct resilience profiles. We will then employ multivariate logistic regression to examine the association of these profiles with specific protective factors (e.g., supportive adult relationships, school engagement, cognitive style) measured by ACMS items, controlling for abuse characteristics and demographics.
proposals.contribution: This research will move beyond documenting risk to empirically identify the constellation of factors associated with exceptional resilience following severe maltreatment. It will provide a nuanced, evidence-based typology of positive adaptation, offering concrete targets for preventive interventions and post-abuse support that are grounded in the real-world experiences of resilient youth.
proposals.novelty: The study innovatively applies Finkelhor's established survey methodology to explicitly model the inverse relationship—high functioning after severe abuse—rather than focusing solely on pathology. It integrates the construct clarification seen in his sibling abuse work with the comprehensive correlates approach of the ACMS to systematically dissect resilience, a less studied counterpart to victimization dynamics.

proposals.title: Prevalence, Co-occurrence, and Mental Health Correlates of Non-Physical Maltreatment: A Secondary Analysis of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study
proposals.motivation: Historically, child protection research and policy have prioritized physical and sexual abuse, often framing them as the most severe forms of maltreatment. However, emerging evidence suggests psychological and financial abuse may be more prevalent and equally damaging, yet they remain under-investigated in large-scale epidemiological studies. This gap limits our understanding of the full spectrum of harm experienced by children and the development of effective prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that, among a nationally representative sample, the lifetime prevalence of childhood psychological abuse and exposure to financial abuse will exceed that of physical abuse, and that these non-physical forms will demonstrate unique and significant associations with adverse mental health outcomes in adulthood, even after controlling for co-occurring physical and sexual abuse.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) dataset, a pre-2026 national survey with validated items measuring childhood maltreatment and associated emotional/mental health outcomes. Using descriptive statistics, we will calculate and compare the prevalence of psychological abuse (e.g., emotional abuse, witnessing domestic violence) and financial abuse items against physical abuse. We will then employ hierarchical logistic regression models to test the unique contribution of each maltreatment type to outcomes like depression, anxiety, and PTSD, controlling for demographics and co-occurrence.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first national, population-based evidence directly comparing the prevalence and mental health impact of non-physical versus physical abuse forms. It will challenge the implicit hierarchy of maltreatment types in policy and practice. The findings will inform a more comprehensive public health approach to child maltreatment prevention that addresses psychological and financial harms.
proposals.novelty: While the ACMS documented high rates of all maltreatment types, no analysis has explicitly tested the hypothesis that non-physical forms are more prevalent than physical violence or parsed their unique predictive power for adult mental health within this robust dataset. This moves beyond documenting co-occurrence to a direct challenge of established priorities.

proposals.title: Profiles and Pathways: A Mixed-Methods Study of the Characteristics, Motivations, and Emotional States of Individuals Who Have Committed Child Maltreatment
proposals.motivation: Despite decades of research on child victimization, direct, systematic study of individuals who have committed abuse remains rare and methodologically challenging. This creates a critical gap in prevention science, as effective interventions require understanding the perpetrator's perspective, including their psychological profiles, situational stressors, and justifications. Building on the methodological foundation of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), this research addresses this gap by directly investigating the characteristics of abusers.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals who have committed child maltreatment will exhibit distinct, measurable profiles characterized by specific combinations of psychological traits (e.g., emotional dysregulation, cognitive distortions), adverse childhood experiences, and situational stressors that differentiate them from non-perpetrating individuals from similar backgrounds.
proposals.method: We will recruit a purposive sample of 150-200 self-identified individuals who have committed acts of child maltreatment (physical, emotional, sexual, neglect, exposure to domestic violence) via community and online outreach, with safeguards for ethical participation. Using a convergent mixed-methods design, participants will complete standardized assessments (e.g., Trauma Symptom Inventory, Difficulties in Emotion Regulation Scale, hostile attribution bias tasks) and a structured interview. Quantitative data will be analyzed using latent class analysis (LCA) to identify distinct perpetrator profiles. Qualitative interview data on motivations and emotional states will be thematically analyzed and then integrated with the LCA profiles to provide a nuanced understanding of each class.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate the first empirically derived typology of child maltreatment perpetrators based on direct data, moving beyond clinical or offender samples. It will provide crucial evidence for developing targeted, profile-specific prevention and intervention programs. The findings will also inform risk assessment tools and contribute to theoretical models of maltreatment etiology by clarifying the interplay between individual characteristics and situational dynamics.
proposals.novelty: This study directly inverts the dominant victim-centric research paradigm to focus on perpetrator characteristics using a rigorous, mixed-methods approach with a community-based sample, rather than relying solely on incarcerated or clinical populations. It integrates the study of emotional regulation and cognitive items—key recent methodological themes—with a focus on the understudied 'inverse problem' of abuse etiology.

proposals.title: Testing the Victim-to-Offender Overlap: A Causal Analysis of Shared Etiology vs. Direct Causation in Maltreatment and Delinquency
proposals.motivation: The well-documented victim-offender overlap often leads to the assumption that victimization, especially child maltreatment, is a primary causal driver of later criminality. This assumption heavily influences intervention policy. However, pre-2026 developmental psychopathology and life-course theories suggest shared underlying familial, community, and emotional dysregulation factors may cause both outcomes, challenging the direct causal narrative and necessitating a rigorous test.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the statistical association between child maltreatment victimization and subsequent delinquent/criminal behavior is substantially attenuated or eliminated when controlling for a constellation of shared etiological factors, including family violence exposure, caregiver substance abuse, poverty, and early emotional and behavioral dysregulation.
proposals.method: We will conduct secondary data analysis on the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) dataset, which includes detailed items on maltreatment types, family characteristics, and emotional/behavioral items. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will test a model where latent variables for shared familial adversity and early dysregulation predict both maltreatment victimization and later delinquency. We will compare model fit to a direct causation model and use path analysis to quantify mediation effects, controlling for demographics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, empirically tested model of the victim-offender link, moving beyond simple correlation. It will inform more precise prevention strategies by identifying whether interventions should target maltreatment sequelae specifically or the shared upstream risk factors. The findings will contribute directly to debates on family violence, sibling aggression, and the interpretation of national survey data like the ACMS.
proposals.novelty: While the victim-offender overlap is known, explicitly modeling and testing shared etiology against direct causation using a comprehensive, nationally representative child maltreatment dataset (ACMS) is novel. It integrates Finkelhor's work on abuse types and constructs with advanced causal modeling to challenge a predominant assumption in the field.

proposals.title: Modeling the Social Contagion of Poly-Victimization in Adolescent Networks: An Epidemiological Approach
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, research by Finkelhor and others established that victimization (e.g., bullying, cyberstalking, sibling aggression) is not randomly distributed but clusters in individuals (poly-victimization) and social networks. Understanding the mechanisms of this spread is critical for prevention. Applying epidemiological models, which have successfully mapped the diffusion of health behaviors and emotions in networks, offers a novel framework to quantify how victimization transmits through adolescent peer environments, moving beyond static correlational studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to victimized peers increases an adolescent's risk of subsequent victimization, and that this dynamic can be accurately modeled using modified Susceptible-Exposed-Infected-Recovered (SEIR) compartmental models, where 'infection' represents experiencing a new victimization event.
proposals.method: We will leverage data from a longitudinal survey of Australian adolescents (building on ACMS methodology) mapping friendship networks and victimization experiences (emotional, physical, sexual, cyber). We will adapt network-based SEIR models, treating victimization status as the infectious state. Key parameters (transmission rate, recovery rate) will be estimated using stochastic simulation and approximate Bayesian computation (ABC). Models will be validated by comparing predicted diffusion patterns with observed longitudinal data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimates of victimization transmission rates in peer networks, identifying key social vectors for intervention. It will advance the field by formally testing the 'contagion' hypothesis of violence and poly-victimization, offering a dynamic, systems-level complement to traditional risk-factor analyses. The findings will directly inform peer-focused prevention programs.
proposals.novelty: While epidemiological models have been applied to health behaviors, their application to the spread of violent victimization itself is novel. This approach moves beyond identifying static risk factors to modeling the dynamic, peer-driven process of victimization accumulation over time.

proposals.title: Interacting Pathways to Safety: A Multi-Level Analysis of Economic and Social Factors in the Decline of Child Victimization
proposals.motivation: Longitudinal data from the U.S. and other nations (e.g., the Australian Child Maltreatment Study) indicate a significant decline in many forms of child victimization since the 1990s. While macro-level factors like economic prosperity and targeted social interventions (e.g., prevention programs, improved child protection systems) have been proposed as separate explanatory drivers, their potential synergistic or antagonistic interactions remain empirically untested. Understanding these dynamics is critical for efficiently allocating resources and sustaining the decline across different maltreatment types.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the decline in child victimization rates is not simply the additive result of independent factors, but is produced by specific interactions, such that the protective effect of economic prosperity is significantly amplified in jurisdictions with higher investment in evidence-based social interventions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of pooled, state-level U.S. data (e.g., NCANDS, NCVS) on child maltreatment and sexual victimization trends from 2000-2025, linked with economic indicators (unemployment, median income) and social intervention metrics (per-capita spending on child welfare, penetration of school-based prevention programs). Using hierarchical linear modeling (HLM) with time-series cross-sectional data, we will test for cross-level interactions between time-varying economic factors and time-invariant state intervention profiles. The analysis will specifically model interaction terms to determine if slopes of decline differ based on the conjunction of economic and intervention variables.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field beyond cataloguing correlates of decline to modeling the causal pathways through which macro-factors operate. It will provide actionable evidence on whether economic gains and social policies work in tandem or independently, offering a more nuanced guide for policy and prevention strategy. The findings will also refine theoretical models of societal change in family violence.
proposals.novelty: While Finkelhor's "Four Preconditions" model explains individual perpetration, this study applies a socio-ecological lens to macro-temporal trends, testing interaction effects that have been theorized but not rigorously quantified. It innovatively bridges developmental victimology with public health and economic sociology methods.

proposals.title: Robustness-First Causal Inference: A Multi-Method Framework for Bounding the Contributions of Social and Policy Factors to Declines in Child Maltreatment
proposals.motivation: The documented declines in child sexual abuse and physical maltreatment since the 1990s represent a major but poorly understood public health success. Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's own work, has identified a suite of plausible contributing factors (e.g., public awareness campaigns, changes in family structure, economic trends, therapeutic and justice system interventions), but analyses have been largely correlational. The field lacks a rigorous, causal methodology to adjudicate between these competing explanations, hindering the efficient allocation of prevention resources.
proposals.hypothesis: The apparent contribution of any single proposed factor (e.g., improved child protection systems, economic improvement) to the observed decline in child victimization is not a fixed weight but a bounded range, and the robustness of a factor's estimated effect varies significantly based on modeling assumptions and the presence of unmeasured confounders like community social cohesion.
proposals.method: The framework begins by formally specifying Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) for each major explanatory theory, clarifying required assumptions. Using longitudinal data (e.g., from the Australian Child Maltreatment Study or similar U.S. surveys), we will deploy an ensemble of complementary estimators: (1) Structural Equation Models (SEM) for latent constructs and mediation; (2) Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation (TMLE) and Double Machine Learning (DML) using gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost) for flexible, non-parametric adjustment of high-dimensional confounders; and (3) agent-based simulations to model social diffusion of protective norms. Sensitivity analyses will quantify how estimates change under varied plausible scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first bounded, robustness-focused assessment of the causes behind declining child victimization, moving beyond point estimates to show which factors' contributions are stable across methods and which are highly model-dependent. It will deliver a formal, replicable causal inference framework specifically designed for complex social epidemiology problems in child maltreatment research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work lists associated factors, this proposal innovates by rigorously applying modern causal inference and adversarial robustness-testing principles from other fields to child victimization trends, explicitly treating competing explanations as causal hypotheses to be stress-tested rather than correlates to be weighted.

proposals.title: Decomposing the Decline: A Multi-Factor Analysis of Trends in Child Victimization Using Australian and U.S. Longitudinal Data
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's work, documents significant declines in several forms of child victimization (e.g., sexual abuse, physical assault) since the 1990s. However, the field lacks a rigorous, quantitative assessment of the relative contributions of competing explanatory factors (e.g., improved child protection systems, economic trends, demographic shifts, changes in parenting norms). Understanding which factors are most strongly associated with the decline is critical for efficiently targeting future prevention resources and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of socio-economic, demographic, and child protection policy factors will explain the observed declines, but that their relative weights will differ significantly by type of victimization (e.g., sexual vs. physical abuse) and jurisdiction.
proposals.method: We will apply hierarchical linear modeling and Shapley value decomposition to longitudinal, state/territory-level data from Australia (leveraging ACMS methodology and associated items) and the U.S. (National Child Abuse and Neglect Data System, NCANDS). The analysis will model annual victimization rates as a function of time-varying covariates (e.g., unemployment, poverty, police presence, child protection expenditures, public awareness campaign intensity). The Shapley value approach will statistically partition the explained variance in the decline, assigning a weight to each proposed factor's unique contribution.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical quantification of the drivers behind the historic decline in child victimization, moving beyond qualitative speculation. It will offer concrete evidence to guide policy by identifying which levers (economic, protective, normative) have been most potent. The cross-national comparison will test the generalizability of findings across different welfare and legal systems.
proposals.novelty: While declines have been documented, no study has used advanced decomposition techniques like Shapley values on longitudinal administrative and survey data to statistically apportion credit among multiple, co-occurring societal explanations. The direct comparison of Australian and U.S. contexts is also novel.

proposals.title: Testing the Persistence of Explanatory Factors: A Multi-Factor Analysis of Child Victimization Trends from 2004 to 2024
proposals.motivation: Finkelhor and Jones's seminal 2004 review identified key factors (e.g., new policies, economic trends, demographic shifts) explaining the substantial declines in child victimization from the 1990s. However, the persistence of these explanatory factors and the trajectory of victimization rates in the subsequent two decades remain empirically untested. Understanding whether these factors continued to drive trends is critical for evaluating the long-term efficacy of prevention policies and for anticipating future challenges in child protection.
proposals.hypothesis: The multi-factor explanatory model developed for pre-2004 declines in child victimization will show differential persistence post-2004, with some factors (e.g., policing strategies) continuing to correlate with declines, while others (e.g., psychopharmacology) will show diminished or altered associations, particularly for newer forms of victimization like cyberstalking and online-facilitated abuse.
proposals.method: This study will replicate and extend the 2004 multi-factor review methodology. We will systematically compile and analyze U.S. national trend data (2004-2024) from sources like the NCVS, NIBRS, and the Youth Risk Behavior Survey for physical abuse, sexual abuse, neglect, and emerging cyber-victimizations. For each trend, we will assess the concurrent trajectories of previously identified explanatory factors (e.g., incarceration rates, opioid prescriptions, economic indicators) and new potential factors (e.g., social media penetration, state-level ACEs screening mandates) using time-series analyses and Granger-causality tests to evaluate persistent and shifting associations.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first comprehensive, longitudinal test of the dominant explanatory model for child victimization trends, offering crucial evidence on which prevention strategies have sustained impact. It will update the empirical foundation for policy by integrating data on digital-era victimization and contemporary societal changes, directly informing initiatives like the Australian Child Maltreatment Study's focus on correlates and modernizing frameworks for understanding sibling and family violence.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established correlates of past declines, this study uniquely applies a rigorous, historical model to contemporary data to test its explanatory limits, explicitly bridging the pre- and post-digital eras in victimization. It moves beyond documenting prevalence to dynamically modeling the evolving relationship between societal factors and multiple forms of child maltreatment over a critical 20-year period.

proposals.title: A Comparative Historical Ecology of Declining Child Victimization: Triangulating Multi-National Data to Identify Cross-Cultural Drivers
proposals.motivation: While the U.S. has documented significant declines in child physical and sexual abuse since the 1990s, it is unclear if this pattern is unique or part of a broader international trend. Understanding the drivers of this decline is critical for sustaining progress and guiding global policy. Pre-2026, studies like the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) provide rich national data, but a systematic, multi-national comparison of the interacting factors behind these trends is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that declines in child victimization across developed nations are driven by a common, interacting constellation of socio-legal factors (e.g., diffusion of prevention programs, specific legal reforms, changes in public awareness), and that divergent national trends can be explained by variations in the timing and intensity of these drivers.
proposals.method: We will adapt historical ecology's 'multiple evidence base' approach. For selected nations (US, UK, Canada, Australia, Sweden), we will compile and align longitudinal data streams: national victimization surveys (e.g., NCANDS, ACMS), child protection system records, legislative histories, and key socio-economic indicators (e.g., inequality, family structure). Using a structured qualitative comparative analysis (QCA) framework, we will construct synchronized, multi-factor timelines to identify necessary and sufficient conditions associated with periods of decline, stagnation, or divergence. The analysis will test for temporal precedence and co-occurrence of potential drivers across contexts.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond single-country explanations to provide a robust, multi-causal model of how child victimization declines. It will identify which policy-relevant factors are most consistently associated with positive trends across different cultural and institutional settings. The methodological framework will offer a new template for integrating disparate administrative, survey, and historical data in social science research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the integrative, multi-evidence methodology of historical ecology to a contemporary social problem, and in systematically testing for common drivers across nations rather than assuming U.S. trends are normative. This moves past descriptive comparisons to a causal, configurational analysis.

proposals.title: Modeling the Dynamics of Child Maltreatment: A System Dynamics Approach Using Adapted Compartmental Models
proposals.motivation: Despite extensive research on the prevalence and correlates of child maltreatment, our understanding of its dynamic, population-level trajectories remains limited. Pre-2026, large-scale studies like the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) provide crucial cross-sectional data on prevalence and associated factors, but they cannot simulate how changes in risk and protective factors interact over time to affect overall rates. A dynamic, systems-level model is needed to evaluate the long-term and system-wide impact of prevention and intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting epidemiological compartment models (e.g., SIR/SEIR) to child victimization will create a valid virtual testbed. We hypothesize that this model will reveal non-linear dynamics and critical leverage points where interventions (e.g., school programs, trauma-informed care) can most effectively reduce population-level maltreatment prevalence and increase resilience.
proposals.method: We will develop a system dynamics model using software like Vensim or Stella. Drawing on pre-2026 data (e.g., ACMS prevalence rates, longitudinal studies on risk/protective factors), we will define key stocks (e.g., Susceptible/At-Risk, Currently Victimized, In Recovery/Resilient) and flows between them. Transition rates will be parameterized as functions of modifiable factors (e.g., parental substance abuse rates, availability of prevention services). Feedback loops, such as the impact of prior victimization on re-victimization risk, will be formally incorporated. The model will be calibrated and validated against historical trend data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational tool for simulating the population-level impact of policy and programmatic interventions before implementation. It will advance the field by moving beyond static correlational analysis to a dynamic systems perspective, identifying high-yield intervention targets. The model will formalize Finkelhor's conceptual work on victimization dynamics and feedback loops into a testable, quantitative framework.
proposals.novelty: While system dynamics has been applied to public health, its application to child maltreatment, specifically integrating detailed survey data (like ACMS items) to parameterize victimization and recovery pathways, is novel. This approach formally treats victimization as a dynamic population process with feedback, unlike most statistical models which focus on individual-level risk.

proposals.title: Spatial Heterogeneity in the Decline of Child Maltreatment: A Geographically Weighted Regression Analysis of Community-Level Protective and Risk Factors
proposals.motivation: While national surveys like the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) document overall declines in child victimization, these trends are unlikely uniform across communities. Understanding the local, place-based conditions associated with steeper or shallower declines is critical for targeting prevention resources effectively. This research addresses a gap by moving beyond national averages to analyze the spatial dynamics of child maltreatment trends, integrating core concepts from CPTED about how environments influence victimization risk.
proposals.hypothesis: Declines in child maltreatment are spatially clustered, and the strength of association between decline rates and key community factors (e.g., access to family support services, economic deprivation, social cohesion) varies significantly across geographic regions, revealing distinct local pathways to prevention success.
proposals.method: We will apply spatial econometrics to analyze child maltreatment data aggregated to a small-area level (e.g., census tracts or postcodes). After testing for global spatial autocorrelation using Moran's I, we will employ Geographically Weighted Regression (GWR) as the primary analytical technique. GWR will model local parameter estimates for independent variables (drawn from census and administrative data on socio-economics, service density, and community characteristics), allowing the relationships between these factors and maltreatment decline rates to vary across the study landscape, identifying localized hotspots of resilience or vulnerability.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first fine-grained, spatial map of child victimization decline patterns, identifying specific communities where prevention has been most and least effective. It will advance the field by demonstrating which community-level factors are most strongly associated with improvement in different types of locales, moving beyond one-size-fits-all models to inform spatially targeted policy and resource allocation.
proposals.novelty: This proposal novelly adapts spatial criminological methods (CPTED, GWR) predominantly used for analyzing crime hotspots to the study of *declines* in child victimization, a positive trend. It integrates Finkelhor's focus on child maltreatment types and correlates with advanced spatial analysis rarely applied in this specific subfield pre-2026.

proposals.title: Mapping Community-Level Psychiatric Medication Access and Child Maltreatment: A Geospatial Pharmacoepidemiological Analysis of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study Data
proposals.motivation: Child maltreatment is a major public health problem with well-documented geographic clustering (hotspots). Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's work on the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), has identified individual and family risk factors, but the community-level role of mental healthcare access in shaping these spatial patterns is understudied. This gap limits the ability to design targeted, place-based prevention strategies that leverage existing healthcare infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that geographic areas (e.g., Statistical Area Level 2) with higher rates of appropriate psychiatric medication utilization (for conditions like parental depression or child ADHD) will exhibit significantly lower spatial clustering of child maltreatment, after controlling for socioeconomic and demographic confounders.
proposals.method: We will link de-identified ACMS data on maltreatment prevalence and types with geocoded, aggregated pharmaceutical dispensing data from Australia's PBS (Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme) for relevant medication classes (e.g., antidepressants, ADHD medications). Using Geographic Information Systems (GIS), we will conduct spatial autocorrelation (Global and Local Moran's I) to identify maltreatment hotspots. We will then employ spatial regression models, specifically Spatial Lag or Spatial Error models, to test the association between medication access rates and hotspot intensity, controlling for ACMS-derived community factors (e.g., disadvantage, social cohesion).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first population-level, geospatial evidence linking psychiatric medication access to community rates of child victimization. It will move the field from individual-level mechanisms to informing public health intervention, identifying specific regions where improving pharmacological treatment access could maximally disrupt maltreatment cycles. The findings will directly inform targeted resource allocation for mental health services as a community prevention strategy.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely fuses geospatial epidemiology, pharmacoepidemiology, and child victimization research—a novel integration not present in pre-2026 literature. It applies spatial regression techniques to the rich, nationally representative ACMS dataset to answer a new community-level question about healthcare as a protective ecological factor.

proposals.title: Geospatial Analysis of Declining Child Victimization: Identifying Local Clusters and Correlates Using Australian Child Maltreatment Study Data
proposals.motivation: While national declines in child victimization are documented, the pattern is unlikely uniform. Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's work on the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), highlights the need to move beyond national averages to understand local dynamics. Identifying geographic clusters where declines are lagging or accelerating can reveal critical community-level risk and protective factors, informing targeted prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: Declines in child maltreatment prevalence are spatially clustered, and these clusters are significantly associated with local levels of socio-economic, service provision, and community cohesion factors.
proposals.method: We will apply spatial epidemiology methods to ACMS data, geocoding participant locations. Using SaTScan software, we will perform retrospective space-time scan statistics to detect statistically significant clusters of high and low decline rates for multiple maltreatment types. Identified clusters will be correlated with local area data (e.g., census socio-economic indexes, density of family support services, police-reported crime) using spatial regression models (e.g., Geographically Weighted Regression) to test for associations.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained geospatial map of child victimization trends in Australia, moving from national description to local explanation. It will identify specific communities in need of enhanced intervention and generate hypotheses about which local factors are most strongly associated with positive change, directly informing resource allocation and community-level prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While geospatial analysis is common in criminology and epidemiology, its application to nationally representative, self-reported child maltreatment trend data is novel. This approach integrates Finkelhor's expertise in child victimization measurement with advanced spatial statistics to move beyond correlating factors with prevalence to correlating them with *change in prevalence*.

proposals.title: Disaggregating the Crime Decline: A Micro-Environmental Test of Distinct Causal Pathways for Child Maltreatment, Sibling Aggression, and Cybersticking
proposals.motivation: The significant declines in many forms of crime and victimization since the 1990s, including child sexual abuse, are a major social phenomenon. Prevailing macro-level theories (e.g., improved guardianship, changing norms) often propose a single universal cause, yet these declines may have distinct etiologies. This gap hinders the development of targeted prevention policies, particularly for family violence and cyber-victimization, which require precise, mechanism-based explanations.
proposals.hypothesis: The declines in child sexual abuse, physical sibling aggression, and juvenile cybersticking victimization are driven by distinct, rather than universal, causal mechanisms, which can be identified by examining how macro-level factors differentially alter opportunity structures and perpetrator motivations within shared high-risk micro-environments (e.g., specific families, households).
proposals.method: The study employs a comparative, multi-method design nested within the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) framework. We will identify a stratified sample of high-risk micro-environments (households/neighborhood blocks) from ACMS data. Using linked administrative data and in-depth qualitative interviews (informed by Finkelhor's work on victimization characteristics and emotional dynamics), we will construct longitudinal case histories for each micro-unit. We will then apply Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA) and multi-level modeling to test for necessary and sufficient configurations of factors (e.g., parental monitoring, digital access, economic stress, normative attitudes) associated with the presence or absence of decline for each victimization type.
proposals.contribution: This research will move beyond monolithic explanations of crime trends by providing a disaggregated, mechanism-based account of why different harms declined. It will directly inform more nuanced prevention strategies for child protection and youth safety. The study will also advance methodological integration by linking national survey data with micro-environmental causal tracing.
proposals.novelty: It innovatively tests competing macro-theories *within* the same high-risk micro-units, allowing for direct comparison of causal pathways across crime types—a design neither purely macro-comparative nor general micro-risk studies can achieve. It applies this framework to under-studied declines in family and cyber-domain victimizations.

proposals.title: Modeling Historical Surges in Child Victimization: An Epidemiological Framework of Agent, Host, and Environment
proposals.motivation: Historical periods of rising child maltreatment, such as during the expansion of industrial labor or the early internet era, are often explained by single factors, limiting our understanding of their complex dynamics. This gap hinders the development of effective, multi-systemic prevention strategies. Applying a robust, interdisciplinary framework can provide a more holistic diagnostic tool for analyzing past surges to inform future policy.
proposals.hypothesis: Historical surges in child victimization rates can be best explained by the concurrent and interactive change in three epidemiological components: novel or intensified 'agent' vectors (e.g., exploitative technologies), increased 'host' susceptibility in child populations (e.g., via economic deprivation), and permissive 'environmental' conditions (e.g., weakened legal protections).
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods historical case study analysis, focusing on a period like the 1990s rise in online sexual exploitation. Quantitatively, we will adapt compartmental models (e.g., SIR-like models) to analyze time-series data on victimization proxies, treating changes in internet access (agent), youth online population (host), and regulatory frameworks (environment) as parameters. Qualitatively, we will code archival policy documents and media using frameworks from the Australian Child Maltreatment Study to identify associated emotional and contextual items, assessing the relative weight of each component.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, testable model for diagnosing the drivers of child victimization surges, moving the field beyond monocausal explanations. It will yield a methodological framework for analyzing historical and contemporary data to identify tipping points and primary intervention targets for policymakers and child protection systems.
proposals.novelty: While epidemiological models are standard in public health, their structured application to the social phenomenon of child victimization surges is novel. This approach formally integrates technological, demographic, and socio-legal variables into a single interactive model, a significant advance over analyses focusing on correlates in isolation.

proposals.title: Identifying and Tracking Persistent Victimization Subpopulations: A Prospective Cohort Analysis of Non-Declining Maltreatment Trajectories
proposals.motivation: Despite overall declines in some forms of child victimization, cross-sectional data (e.g., from the Australian Child Maltreatment Study) suggests specific subpopulations may experience persistent or non-declining rates. Understanding these 'sticky' contexts—such as certain types of online exploitation or chronic familial neglect—is critical for targeted prevention. Current research, however, relies on retrospective reports or cross-sectional snapshots, limiting causal inference about the mechanisms driving persistence across the life course.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that persistent victimization subpopulations are characterized by specific, modifiable risk factors (e.g., systemic intervention failures, ongoing under-reporting dynamics, or concentrated social disadvantage) that sustain victimization risk from childhood into young adulthood, leading to distinct revictimization and mental health trajectories.
proposals.method: Building on the ACMS methodology, we will first use latent class analysis on its national data to identify subpopulations with high, non-declining risk profiles for specific maltreatment types (e.g., emotional abuse, cyberstalking). We will then establish a prospective, multi-wave longitudinal cohort from these identified subpopulations, tracking them over 5 years using validated measures of victimization (Juvenile Victimization Questionnaire), emotional well-being, service engagement, and reporting behaviors. Analysis will employ growth mixture modeling to map trajectories and multi-level regression to test drivers of persistence.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field from identifying static correlates of maltreatment to modeling the dynamic processes that sustain victimization for specific groups. It will provide direct evidence on whether persistence is due to continued under-reporting, revictimization, or failed systemic responses, informing the development of life-course-informed interventions. The findings will extend Finkelhor's work on polyvictimization and sibling aggression by clarifying their longitudinal dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novel fusion of latent subpopulation identification with prospective cohort tracking directly targets the mechanisms of persistence in real time, a significant advance over inferring causality from retrospective or cross-sectional data. It applies a life-course epidemiological framework to the specific problem of non-declining victimization rates.

proposals.title: Resilience and Varied Outcomes: A Latent Profile Analysis of Psychological Functioning Following Self-Reported Child Maltreatment in the Australian Child Maltreatment Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on child maltreatment, including Finkelhor's own work, has robustly established its association with a wide range of psychological pathologies. However, this dominant risk-and-pathology paradigm may inadvertently obscure understanding of resilience and heterogeneous outcomes. It is critical to systematically identify and characterize individuals who report maltreatment but do not report significant impairment, as this can refine etiological models and inform more nuanced prevention and intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant subset of individuals in a nationally representative sample who report experiences of child maltreatment will belong to latent profiles characterized by low levels of concurrent psychological distress and functional impairment. Membership in these resilient profiles will be associated with specific individual, familial, and community-level protective factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct secondary data analysis on the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), a pre-2026 national survey with detailed maltreatment and outcome measures. Using Latent Profile Analysis (LPA), we will model latent subgroups based on key outcome variables (e.g., depression, anxiety, PTSD symptoms, suicidality, substance use). We will then use multinomial logistic regression to test associations between profile membership and potential protective factors (e.g., emotional support, school connectedness, community safety) measured within the ACMS, controlling for maltreatment type, severity, and poly-victimization.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide population-based empirical evidence for the existence and prevalence of resilient outcomes following child maltreatment, moving beyond case studies. It will identify modifiable protective factors associated with positive adaptation, offering concrete targets for strength-based clinical and public health interventions. The findings will challenge deterministic models and contribute to a more balanced scientific narrative on maltreatment consequences.
proposals.novelty: While resilience is acknowledged, this study applies a person-centered, latent variable approach to a large, national dataset to quantitatively model resilient subgroups and their correlates, a method not typically applied to the ACMS data for this specific question. It directly tests the assumption of necessary pathology by focusing on the positive tail of the outcome distribution.

proposals.title: Developmental Pathways from Poly-Victimization in Childhood to Adult Functioning: A Latent Class Analysis of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study Data
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's work on poly-victimization and the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), has established that child maltreatment is a potent risk factor for adult psychopathology and dysfunction. However, the field lacks a detailed, lifespan developmental understanding of how specific constellations of abuse types (e.g., sexual, physical, emotional, exposure to domestic violence) and their co-occurrence interact with developmental stage and emotional outcomes to create distinct pathways to adult functioning. This gap limits the precision of prevention and intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct latent classes of maltreatment experiences, defined by type, severity, developmental timing, and associated emotional symptoms, will predict unique profiles of adult functioning (e.g., mental health, relationship quality, occupational stability). Further, we hypothesize that emotional dysregulation in adolescence will be a key mediating mechanism linking these maltreatment classes to adverse adult outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct secondary data analysis of the comprehensive, nationally representative ACMS dataset. Using a developmental psychopathology framework, we will first perform Latent Class Analysis (LCA) to identify subgroups based on maltreatment type, frequency, and perpetrator relationship (e.g., sibling, parent). We will then use Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) to test pathways from these latent classes, through mediators of adolescent emotional and behavioral problems (using ACMS retrospective items on associated emotional impacts), to a range of adult outcome variables.
