Analyses will control for key demographics and social context factors available in the ACMS.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond documenting simple associations to map etiological pathways, identifying high-risk maltreatment profiles and their specific developmental sequalae. It will provide empirical evidence for targeted, developmentally-informed interventions. The use of the rich ACMS data will offer unprecedented national-level insights into these pathways within the Australian context, with implications for global child protection policy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined types of maltreatment, this study uniquely integrates Finkelhor's focus on poly-victimization and relationship contexts with a formal developmental pathways analysis. It applies a lifespan model to retrospective national survey data to test how maltreatment constellations, not just single types, shape long-term trajectories through specific emotional mechanisms.

proposals.title: Family Systems Contexts of Child Maltreatment: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Co-occurrence, Dynamics, and Recovery Using Australian Child Maltreatment Study Data
proposals.motivation: Despite robust evidence on the prevalence and individual correlates of child maltreatment, there is a critical gap in understanding the familial relational contexts in which abuse and recovery occur. Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's work on sibling aggression and cyberstalking, highlights that maltreatment is embedded in family systems, yet most large-scale surveys like the ACMS analyze abuse types as isolated variables. A family systems lens is essential to move beyond siloed risk factors and inform holistic, family-level prevention and intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, measurable family system characteristics—such as communication patterns, boundaries, triangulation, and emotional climate—will significantly predict the co-occurrence of multiple maltreatment types (polyvictimization) and will moderate the relationship between maltreatment exposure and subsequent emotional and behavioral outcomes in children and adolescents.
proposals.method: This secondary analysis will integrate quantitative data from the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) with a new qualitative component. We will develop and validate a brief family systems assessment module (e.g., assessing cohesion, adaptability, conflict resolution) for inclusion in a follow-up ACMS wave. Quantitative analysis will use structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the mediating and moderating roles of family system variables. Concurrently, we will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with a purposive sample of ACMS participants to explore the lived experience of these familial dynamics in relation to abuse and recovery processes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first nationally representative, empirical model linking family systems theory to child maltreatment data, identifying specific relational dynamics that exacerbate risk or promote resilience. It will advance the field by offering actionable targets for family-focused therapeutic interventions and moving etiological models beyond individual and parent-level factors to include systemic interactions.
proposals.novelty: While family systems theory is established in clinical practice, its rigorous application to large-scale, population-level child maltreatment survey data is novel. This study uniquely bridges the gap between clinical family assessment methods and epidemiological survey science to test systemic hypotheses about abuse etiology and recovery.

proposals.title: Neurobiological Correlates of Trauma Response Subtypes in Child Maltreatment: Linking Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) Data with Neuroimaging and Physiological Markers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's work on the ACMS, has established the high prevalence and diverse psychological impacts of child maltreatment. However, the field lacks a granular understanding of how specific types of abuse (e.g., sexual, physical, emotional) and their co-occurrence map onto distinct neurobiological profiles of trauma response. This gap limits the development of biologically-informed, personalized interventions for survivors.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific types and combinations of child maltreatment (as measured by the ACMS) will be associated with distinct, measurable patterns of neurobiological dysregulation, including altered amygdala reactivity to threat cues, dysregulated hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis function, and specific patterns of emotional processing as measured by psychophysiological responses.
proposals.method: This study will recruit a sub-sample of 200 young adult participants (aged 18-25) from the ACMS cohort, stratified by their self-reported maltreatment history (sexual, physical, emotional, neglect, exposure to domestic violence) and associated emotional symptom profiles. Participants will undergo: 1) functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) during an emotional face-processing task to assess amygdala and prefrontal cortex activation; 2) diurnal salivary cortisol collection to measure HPA axis rhythm; and 3) skin conductance response (SCR) and heart rate variability (HRV) monitoring during standardized emotional recall tasks. Data will be analyzed using multivariate analysis of covariance (MANCOVA) and structural equation modeling (SEM) to test for unique neurobiological signatures associated with maltreatment types, controlling for demographics and current psychopathology.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first large-scale, population-based evidence linking detailed maltreatment typologies from a national survey to multi-modal neurobiological data. It will move the field beyond generic 'trauma' models to identify specific biological pathways disrupted by different abuse experiences. Findings will directly inform the development of more targeted, mechanism-based prevention and treatment strategies.
proposals.novelty: While neurobiological studies of child maltreatment exist, none have leveraged the detailed, population-representative typology data from a study like the ACMS. The novelty lies in testing for differential neurobiological correlates across the specific maltreatment types and combinations that Finkelhor's epidemiological work has shown to have distinct psychological outcomes.

proposals.title: The Unseen Majority: A Population-Based Study of Child Sexual Abuse Experiences Unidentified by Professional Systems
proposals.motivation: Research and policy on child sexual abuse (CSA) overwhelmingly rely on cases identified by child protection, law enforcement, or clinical services. However, prevalence studies consistently show that only a minority of CSA experiences are ever reported to such authorities. This creates a profound knowledge gap, as the characteristics, contexts, and impacts of the vast majority of abuse—what Finkelhor terms the "iceberg" beneath the surface—remain poorly understood, biasing our understanding of the problem and hindering effective prevention and response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CSA experiences never identified by professional systems will differ significantly from identified cases in terms of perpetrator relationship, abuse characteristics, disclosure patterns, and associated emotional and behavioral outcomes, and that these differences will persist even after controlling for demographic factors.
proposals.method: Leveraging the methodology of the landmark Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), we will conduct a secondary analysis of its national survey data. We will use a series of logistic regression models to compare the subset of respondents who reported CSA victimization that was never reported to authorities (e.g., police, child protection, doctors) against those whose experiences were professionally identified. Key dependent variables will include detailed abuse items (e.g., type, frequency, perpetrator), emotional and behavioral outcomes measured by validated scales, and disclosure characteristics. Analyses will control for demographic variables to isolate the unique correlates of non-identification.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, population-based profile of the most common form of CSA—that which remains outside official systems. It will challenge the field's reliance on identified cases as representative, inform more accurate public health models of CSA, and identify specific barriers to disclosure and help-seeking that can be targeted by prevention programs.
proposals.novelty: While the ACMS documented national prevalence, this proposal innovatively uses its data to systematically contrast identified and unidentified abuse experiences, moving beyond simple prevalence to analyze the substantive differences within the victim population that have major implications for theory and practice.

proposals.title: Pathways to Perpetration: A Retrospective Analysis of Developmental and Emotional Correlates in a National Cohort
proposals.motivation: Despite decades of research quantifying victimization, primary prevention requires shifting focus upstream to understand how perpetrators develop. Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's work on sibling aggression and cyberstalking, identifies perpetrator behaviors but lacks integrated, population-level data on the developmental and emotional pathways leading to the initiation of child maltreatment and abuse. This gap limits the design of effective early interruption strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Retrospective self-reports from adults who acknowledge perpetrating child-directed abuse will reveal distinct, early-life developmental pathways characterized by specific emotional deficits, exposure to violence, and maladaptive coping strategies, which differ significantly from the pathways of non-perpetrators.
proposals.method: This secondary analysis will utilize the rich, pre-existing data from the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS). We will identify the subset of respondents who self-report perpetrating abuse (e.g., physical, emotional, sexual, exposure to domestic violence) against children. Using latent class analysis, we will model distinct perpetrator pathways based on retrospective items covering childhood experiences, emotional regulation, peer associations, and early behavioral problems. Multinomial logistic regression will then test associations between these pathway classes and current emotional/psychological outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first nationally representative typologies of perpetrator development, moving beyond victim counts to actionable risk trajectories. It will directly inform the design of targeted, developmentally-sensitive prevention programs aimed at interrupting these pathways before abuse occurs.
proposals.novelty: While the ACMS was designed to assess victimization, this proposal innovatively leverages its data to model perpetrator etiology, applying Finkelhor's conceptual work on constructs and dynamics to the understudied question of origin. It inverts the analytical lens of a major national survey to answer a primary prevention question.

proposals.title: Defining and Measuring Coercion in Child Sexual Abuse: A Focus on Exploitative Non-Force Dynamics in Age and Authority Relationships
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research and policy on child sexual abuse (CSA) often emphasizes overt force or threats, potentially overlooking the profound coercive power inherent in age differences and caretaking roles. This gap is critical because, as Finkelhor's work on constructs and sibling abuse suggests, non-force dynamics can constitute severe family violence. The Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) provides a methodological foundation for measuring such nuanced experiences, but specific analysis of exploitative non-force relationships is needed to inform prevention and intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of CSA victimization reported by young adults involves exploitative dynamics based on age disparity or authority, rather than overt force, and that these experiences are associated with distinct emotional and mental health outcomes compared to force-based abuse.
proposals.method: This study will conduct a secondary analysis of the ACMS data, focusing on respondents reporting CSA. We will develop a new measurement scale of 'exploitative non-force coercion' items, building on existing ACMS items about perpetrator relationship, age difference, and caretaking roles. Using latent class analysis (LCA), we will identify distinct profiles of CSA experiences (e.g., force-based, authority-based exploitation, peer exploitation). Multivariate logistic regression will then be employed to examine associations between these latent classes and emotional outcomes (e.g., anxiety, depression, PTSD symptoms) measured in the ACMS, controlling for demographic covariates.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide an empirical basis for refining the construct of coercion in CSA to include exploitative non-force dynamics, moving beyond a binary force/no-force model. It will quantify the prevalence and specific emotional correlates of such abuse, offering crucial evidence for legal, clinical, and public health frameworks to better recognize and respond to this under-studied form of victimization.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted the role of authority, this study uniquely operationalizes and measures a spectrum of coercive dynamics in CSA by statistically modeling victim-reported experiences of exploitation without overt force, directly testing its differential impact using a large, national dataset.

proposals.title: Cohort-Specific Risk and Protective Factors for Child Maltreatment: A Demographic Analysis of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) Data
proposals.motivation: While national surveys like the ACMS have established population-level prevalence of child maltreatment, a critical gap remains in understanding how risk and protective factors vary across distinct demographic cohorts (e.g., birth cohorts, gender, socioeconomic strata). Pre-2026, most analyses aggregate data, potentially masking cohort-specific dynamics essential for targeted prevention. This study addresses the need for granular, population-level insights to move beyond one-size-fits-all intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific constellations of risk and protective factors for child maltreatment (including sexual abuse, physical abuse, emotional abuse, neglect, and exposure to domestic violence) will differ significantly across demographic cohorts defined by birth decade, gender, and socioeconomic status during childhood.
proposals.method: We will conduct secondary analysis of the ACMS dataset, applying demographic cohort analysis techniques. Cohorts will be defined by birth decade (e.g., 1960s-2000s), gender, and childhood socioeconomic quintile. Using multinomial logistic regression and classification and regression tree (CART) algorithms, we will model the association between a comprehensive set of items (e.g., parental mental health, family structure, community cohesion) and maltreatment outcomes within each cohort. Analyses will identify both universal and cohort-unique factor patterns.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce the first fine-grained, cohort-specific map of maltreatment correlates in Australia, directly informing the development of demographically tailored prevention programs. It will extend Finkelhor's work on clarifying constructs by applying them to cohort dynamics and contribute methodological innovation to the analysis of large-scale victimization surveys.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying formal demographic cohort analysis—common in epidemiology—to the ACMS data to disaggregate risk/protective factors, moving beyond aggregate population models. This approach is non-obvious in child maltreatment research, which has historically focused on individual or family-level predictors rather than cohort-level ecological analysis.

proposals.title: Mapping the Perpetrator Ecology: A Network Analysis of Multi-Victim Child Maltreatment in Australian Contexts
proposals.motivation: Child maltreatment research has historically centered on victim experiences and outcomes, leaving a critical gap in understanding the social and situational ecologies of perpetrators. This is a significant limitation for prevention, as perpetrators who affect multiple children represent a disproportionate source of harm. Building on the foundational prevalence data from the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), this study shifts focus to the networks and contexts that enable multi-victim offending.
proposals.hypothesis: Perpetrators who affect multiple children operate within distinct, identifiable social and situational ecologies characterized by specific network structures (e.g., positions of trust across multiple families or institutions) and opportunity factors that differ significantly from those of single-victim perpetrators.
proposals.method: This study will conduct secondary analysis of ACMS data, supplemented by targeted qualitative interviews with professionals (e.g., child protection, police). We will employ social network analysis (SNA) to model connections between perpetrators, victims, and opportunity contexts (e.g., schools, clubs, extended families). Statistical analyses, including logistic regression and cluster analysis, will identify factors (e.g., perpetrator roles, emotional grooming tactics, digital access) associated with multi-victim patterns versus single incidents.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first empirical map of multi-victim perpetrator ecologies in Australia, moving beyond victim counts to actionable structural insights. It will directly inform situational crime prevention strategies and professional training by identifying high-risk network configurations and modifiable opportunity structures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work details victim characteristics and single-incident dynamics, this study innovatively applies a network ecology framework to perpetrators, synthesizing Finkelhor's pre-2026 work on abuse types, emotional dynamics, and cyber-victimization to model the systemic architecture of multi-victim harm.

proposals.title: Mapping the Spatial Ecology of Child Victimization: A Geospatial Analysis of Exposure Hotspots Using Australian Child Maltreatment Study Data
proposals.motivation: Child victimization research has historically focused on individual and family risk factors, with less attention to the geographic patterning of exposure. Understanding these spatial patterns is crucial for targeted prevention and resource allocation. The availability of national, geocoded data from the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) provides a unique opportunity to apply advanced spatial analytics to this public health problem.
proposals.hypothesis: Child victimization exposure (including maltreatment types and poly-victimization) will cluster in non-random geographic hotspots, and these spatial clusters will be significantly associated with area-level socioeconomic and community characteristics, even after controlling for individual-level risk factors.
proposals.method: We will use ACMS geocoded respondent data to conduct hotspot analysis (Getis-Ord Gi* statistic) and cluster detection (Local Moran's I) to identify significant spatial concentrations of different maltreatment types. We will then employ spatial regression models (e.g., geographically weighted regression) to model the relationship between hotspot locations and area-level covariates (e.g., SEIFA indices, housing stress, service density) derived from the Australian Bureau of Statistics. Analyses will control for individual-level variables from the ACMS.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first national, evidence-based maps of child victimization exposure risk in Australia, moving beyond crime data to model self-reported maltreatment. It will identify specific community-level correlates of geographic risk, informing place-based prevention strategies. The methodology will establish a transferable framework for spatial analysis of victimization survey data.
proposals.novelty: While crime mapping is established, its application to comprehensive, self-reported child maltreatment data from a national population survey is novel. The integration of emotional and contextual items from the ACMS with fine-grained spatial statistics moves beyond simple prevalence mapping to model the ecological dynamics of victimization exposure.

proposals.title: Beyond Force: Developing and Validating a Multi-Axis Coercive Tactics Framework (CTF) for Child Sexual Abuse
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's work on polyvictimization and the Australian Child Maltreatment Study, underscores that child sexual abuse (CSA) often occurs without overt physical force, yet legal and clinical frameworks struggle to adequately categorize these non-force mechanisms. This gap hinders accurate risk assessment, legal prosecution, and the tailoring of support services for survivors whose victimization involved complex grooming, emotional manipulation, or authority-based coercion rather than physical violence.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-axis behavioral framework, inductively derived from survivor narratives, will reliably categorize non-force coercive tactics (e.g., grooming, emotional blackmail, authority exploitation) and demonstrate that specific tactic clusters are differentially associated with distinct trauma-related outcomes, such as betrayal trauma, shame, and emotional dysregulation.
proposals.method: A sequential exploratory mixed-methods design will be employed. Phase 1: In-depth qualitative analysis of 40-60 victim/survivor narratives from existing datasets (e.g., ACMS open-text responses) and new interviews, using thematic analysis and constant comparison to inductively construct the Coercive Tactics Framework (CTF). Phase 2: Quantitative validation via a national survey (n=1500) of Australian adults, using items derived from the CTF. Psychometric validation will involve confirmatory factor analysis to test the CTF's structure, and regression models will assess the predictive power of specific tactic axes on trauma outcomes measured by established scales (e.g., Trauma Symptom Checklist).
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first empirically derived, multi-axis typology of non-force coercive mechanisms in CSA, moving beyond binary force/no-force models. It will provide a validated measurement tool for research and forensic use, and offer evidence on how specific coercive pathways link to mental health sequelae, informing more precise intervention strategies.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior typologies focused on perpetrator characteristics or abuse settings, this framework centers on the granular, sequential *behavioral tactics* of coercion as reported by survivors. It integrates Finkelhor's pre-2026 focus on abuse dynamics and emotional items with a novel methodological approach to map the full spectrum of coercive harm.

proposals.title: Developing and Piloting a Modular, Survivor-Informed Methodological Framework for Child Sexual Abuse Prevalence Research
proposals.motivation: Child sexual abuse (CSA) research methodologies, including the items and emotional approaches used in major surveys like the Australian Child Maltreatment Study, often lack standardization and can inadvertently re-traumatize participants. This creates variability in prevalence estimates and limits comparability across studies, hindering effective policy and intervention development. A participatory, adaptive framework is urgently needed to improve methodological rigor and ethical safety.
proposals.hypothesis: A modular methodological framework, co-developed and iteratively refined by a consortium of researchers, clinicians, and survivors, will produce measurement tools that are more psychometrically robust, ethically sensitive, and capable of generating more valid and comparable prevalence data than traditional top-down approaches.
proposals.method: The project will establish a consortium to govern a 'Methodological Commons.' We will first conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis of pre-2026 CSA measurement items (e.g., from JVQ, ACMS) to identify core constructs and problematic wording. Using participatory design workshops with survivors and experts, we will then develop and psychometrically test alternative modular item sets for different contexts (e.g., online, clinical). Comparative testing will use randomized survey experiments to evaluate measures on criteria of disclosure rates, emotional distress, and criterion validity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the field with a publicly accessible, living repository of vetted, ethically-informed methodological modules for CSA research. It will establish a new model for survivor-engaged methodological innovation, directly improving the quality, comparability, and ethical foundation of future prevalence studies and their associated correlates.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond critiquing existing measures to actively building a pluralistic, adaptive system for methodological evolution, governed by a consortium that centralizes the expertise of survivors—a departure from researcher-dominated standard-setting. It operationalizes 'rigorous pluralism' through comparative testing cycles.

proposals.title: Developing the CSA-RR (Child Sexual Abuse Research Reporting) Framework: A Delphi Consensus Study to Establish Standardized Reporting Guidelines
proposals.motivation: Research on child sexual abuse (CSA) intervention is critical for evidence-based policy and practice, yet the field is hampered by inconsistent and incomplete reporting of studies. This inconsistency, as seen in variable definitions of abuse and outcomes, limits the ability to synthesize findings, assess methodological rigor, and compare the efficacy of interventions. A standardized reporting framework, analogous to CONSORT in clinical trials, is urgently needed to enhance transparency, reproducibility, and the cumulative growth of knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, multi-stage Delphi consensus process with international CSA research experts will yield a core set of mandatory and recommended reporting items, creating a CSA-RR framework that significantly improves the completeness and transparency of published intervention research.
proposals.method: The project will employ a three-round modified Delphi method. First, a steering committee (including the PI) will draft an initial item pool based on a systematic review of CSA intervention literature and existing reporting guidelines (e.g., CONSORT, TREND). Second, a panel of 30-40 international experts (researchers, clinicians, ethicists) will rate each item's importance for inclusion on a 9-point Likert scale over multiple anonymous rounds, with controlled feedback. Third, consensus will be defined a priori (e.g., ≥70% panel rating 7-9). The final framework will be structured with flowcharts and checklists tailored to different CSA research designs (e.g., RCTs, quasi-experimental, service evaluations).
proposals.contribution: This work will directly produce the first consensus-based, field-specific reporting standard (CSA-RR) for CSA intervention research. It will provide journal editors, reviewers, and funders with a tool to mandate higher reporting quality, ultimately enabling more robust meta-analyses, clearer policy implications, and faster translation of research into effective practice to reduce victimization and its associated emotional consequences.
proposals.novelty: While reporting guidelines exist in medicine, their adaptation to the unique ethical and methodological challenges of CSA research—such as defining cases, recruiting hidden populations, and measuring sensitive outcomes—is novel. This project moves beyond simply endorsing generic guidelines to creating a bespoke standard grounded in expert consensus from the victimization research community.

proposals.title: Estimating the True Prevalence of Child Sexual Abuse in Australia: A Multiple Systems Estimation Analysis Using Administrative and Survey Data
proposals.motivation: Accurate prevalence estimates for child sexual abuse (CSA) are critical for policy and resource allocation but are notoriously difficult to obtain due to under-reporting and the 'dark figure' of hidden cases. Existing single-source estimates, from official records or retrospective surveys, are known to be incomplete and potentially biased. A more robust, multi-source statistical estimate is needed to inform the scale of the problem, particularly in light of recent national data collection efforts like the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS).
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Multiple Systems Estimation (MSE) to multiple, independent administrative and survey lists of CSA cases will yield a statistically rigorous and significantly higher total prevalence estimate than any single source, revealing the true scope of victimization, including cases unknown to any official system.
proposals.method: We will utilize de-identified, linked data from four key sources in an Australian jurisdiction: 1) child protection substantiations, 2) police reports, 3) hospital/health records with relevant ICD codes, and 4) victim-identified cases from the ACMS survey. Using probabilistic matching on demographic variables, we will create an overlap table of cases across these lists. We will then fit log-linear models (e.g., using the R package `Rcapture`) to this table to estimate the number of victims not appearing on any list, thereby generating a total population estimate with confidence intervals, while testing for dependencies between data sources.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first MSE-based national estimate of CSA prevalence, moving beyond descriptive counts to a model-based quantification of the hidden population. It will directly inform the interpretation of the ACMS findings by contextualizing survey data within the broader systems landscape. The methodology will establish a replicable framework for ongoing surveillance and for estimating the prevalence of other hard-to-measure forms of child maltreatment.
proposals.novelty: While MSE is established in human rights and epidemiology (e.g., for estimating conflict deaths or drug use), its application to child sexual abuse prevalence estimation using a combination of national survey data and multiple administrative lists is novel. This approach innovatively bridges ecological statistical methods with the complex, multi-system reality of child victimization data.

proposals.title: Leveraging Trust: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Non-Contact Sexual Exploitation Dynamics in Caretaking Relationships
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), has established the prevalence of child maltreatment but often categorizes non-contact sexual harms (e.g., coerced image production) as distinct, lower-severity acts. This framing overlooks how these acts are strategically deployed within the unique power dynamics of caretaking relationships, which provide unparalleled access, trust, and means for coercion. The rise of digital communication and the need to clarify constructs of family violence, as in Finkelhor's work on sibling aggression, demands a focused investigation into how these relational architectures enable exploitation beyond physical contact.
proposals.hypothesis: Non-contact sexual exploitation by caretakers is not a set of isolated behaviors but a coordinated process where the caretaking role itself is weaponized; the mechanisms of grooming and coercion will vary systematically by the type of caretaker relationship (e.g., parent vs. coach) and will be enabled by identifiable systemic gaps in institutional oversight and technological access.
proposals.method: The study will employ a sequential mixed-methods design. First, secondary analysis of ACMS and similar datasets will identify correlates and prevalence patterns of non-contact victimization items associated with different perpetrator types. Second, in-depth qualitative interviews with Australian professionals (e.g., child protection workers, therapists) and analysis of case files will map the specific emotional and relational grooming mechanisms. Data will be analyzed using thematic analysis and logistic regression models to test associations between relationship type, exploitation mechanism, and systemic enablers.
proposals.contribution: This research will generate a refined theoretical model of 'relationship weaponization' in child exploitation, moving beyond act-based definitions. It will produce evidence-based indicators for early detection of grooming within caretaking contexts and offer concrete recommendations for strengthening institutional protocols to disrupt these exploitation architectures before contact abuse occurs.
proposals.novelty: It synthesizes Finkelhor's construct-clarification approach with cyber-victimization insights to analyze non-contact acts as core tools within a relational exploitation process, shifting the focus from the abusive act to the exploitative dynamic of the trusted relationship itself.

proposals.title: Modeling the Social Contagion of Child Maltreatment: An Epidemiological Analysis Using Australian Child Maltreatment Study Data
proposals.motivation: Child maltreatment exhibits significant geographic and social clustering, suggesting potential transmission dynamics akin to infectious diseases. Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's work on polyvictimization and the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), has documented these patterns but lacks a formal modeling framework to explain them. Applying epidemiological models could revolutionize prevention by identifying core 'transmission' mechanisms, such as intergenerational cycles, peer influence, or community norms, moving beyond static risk factor analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatiotemporal distribution of child maltreatment, particularly emotional and sexual abuse, can be effectively modeled using modified Susceptible-Infected-Recovered (SIR) compartmental models, where 'infection' represents the onset of abusive behaviors or victimization risk. We hypothesize that model parameters (e.g., contact rates, recovery) will be significantly associated with specific community-level factors identified in the ACMS.
proposals.method: We will analyze de-identified ACMS data, treating reported maltreatment experiences as 'cases'. Using geographic and network proxies (e.g., household, school district), we will apply agent-based models and spatial SIR models to simulate maltreatment spread. Key steps include: 1) defining 'susceptible' (at-risk), 'infectious' (perpetrating or high-risk environment), and 'recovered' (intervention-affected) states; 2) calibrating transmission rates using ACMS prevalence and associated item data on family dynamics and emotional climate; 3) validating models against observed clustering of abuse types and polyvictimization in the ACMS cohort.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first formal epidemiological framework for understanding child maltreatment as a socially communicable phenomenon. It will generate testable estimates of 'reproduction numbers' for abuse subtypes, identifying which are most 'transmissible'. The findings will directly inform public health-inspired prevention strategies, such as targeting 'superspreader' risk environments or boosting community 'herd immunity' through protective norms.
proposals.novelty: While disease models have been applied to violence (e.g., gun violence), their application to child abuse, using comprehensive national survey data like the ACMS, is novel. This approach moves beyond correlational analysis to model dynamic causation and feedback loops between abuse types, a core interest in Finkelhor's work on co-occurrence.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Trajectories of Health and Service Utilization in a National Cohort of Child Maltreatment Victims: A 10-Year Follow-up Study
proposals.motivation: Despite robust cross-sectional data on child maltreatment prevalence, such as from the landmark Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), there is a critical gap in understanding the long-term, dynamic outcomes for identified victims. Pre-2026 research, including Finkelhor's work on polyvictimization and cyberstalking, underscores that maltreatment types have distinct impacts, but longitudinal data are needed to trace how these outcomes evolve, how reporting to services changes over time, and what factors promote resilience or exacerbate risk across the life course.
proposals.hypothesis: Distinct maltreatment types (sexual abuse, physical abuse, emotional abuse, neglect, exposure to domestic violence) will be associated with unique longitudinal trajectories of mental health, physical health, and service utilization, and these trajectories will be significantly moderated by factors such as revictimization, social support, and socioeconomic context.
proposals.method: This study will employ a prospective longitudinal cohort design, re-contacting a subsample of participants from the ACMS who reported childhood maltreatment. Using stratified random sampling by maltreatment type and demographic factors, we will conduct biennial waves of data collection over 10 years. The method will utilize latent growth curve modeling (LGCM) and group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM) to identify distinct outcome pathways. Analyses will control for baseline covariates from the ACMS and employ time-lagged regressions to examine predictors of service engagement and revictimization.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first nationally representative, longitudinal map of outcomes for child maltreatment victims in Australia, directly informing the lifespan model of victimization. It will identify critical intervention points and risk/protective factors that shape long-term trajectories, offering concrete evidence for timing and targeting of mental health, social, and justice system services.
proposals.novelty: While prior longitudinal studies often follow high-risk or clinical samples, this proposal leverages a unique, pre-established national probability sample (ACMS) of victims, allowing for generalizable findings on the natural history of maltreatment outcomes and filling a major methodological gap highlighted in Finkelhor's work on clarifying constructs and dynamics.

proposals.title: Assessing the Reliability of Parent Proxy Reporting for Child Victimization: A Multi-Type, Multi-Age Analysis of Concordance with Child Self-Report
proposals.motivation: Accurate measurement of child maltreatment prevalence is critical for policy and service provision, yet large-scale surveys like the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) often rely on retrospective adult self-report. For contemporary surveillance, parent proxy reporting is a practical alternative, but its validity across different maltreatment types and child developmental stages remains inadequately tested. Understanding the conditions under which parent reports are reliable is essential for improving epidemiological methods and ensuring children's victimization is not systematically undercounted.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that parent-child concordance will be higher for more observable victimization types (e.g., physical assault) and for younger children, but significantly lower for covert or stigmatized types (e.g., emotional abuse, sexual victimization) and for adolescents, where parent proxy reporting will demonstrate poor reliability.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of existing, pre-2026 datasets containing matched child self-report and parent proxy report on victimization items (e.g., Juvenile Victimization Questionnaire items). Using a multi-group confirmatory factor analysis (MG-CFA) framework, we will model the latent construct of victimization for both reporters. We will then calculate and compare concordance statistics (e.g., intraclass correlation coefficients, kappa) across five victimization types (physical, sexual, emotional abuse, neglect, exposure to domestic violence) and three age groups (5-9, 10-12, 13-17). Analyses will control for family conflict and parent emotional distress.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed, type- and age-specific reliability metrics for parent proxy reporting of child victimization. It will offer empirically grounded guidance for researchers on when proxy reports are sufficient and when child self-report is essential. The findings will directly inform the methodology of future child maltreatment surveillance studies, improving measurement accuracy.
proposals.novelty: While parent-child concordance has been studied for general health, a systematic analysis focused on multiple, distinct victimization types across the full child developmental spectrum is lacking. This study moves beyond a global reliability assessment to identify the precise boundaries of proxy report validity, a novel and practical contribution to measurement science in child maltreatment.

proposals.title: Daily Emotional and Behavioral Trajectories Following Peer and Sibling Victimization: A Micro-Longitudinal Diary Study
proposals.motivation: While cross-sectional surveys like the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) have established population-level prevalence and correlates of child maltreatment, they cannot capture the immediate, dynamic aftermath of victimization events. Understanding the real-time emotional and behavioral sequelae is crucial for identifying critical intervention windows and tailoring support. This gap is particularly salient for under-studied but prevalent forms of family violence, such as sibling aggression, and for technology-facilitated victimization like cyberstalking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific types of victimization (e.g., sibling aggression vs. peer cyberstalking) will produce distinct, time-sensitive patterns of daily emotional dysregulation and behavioral withdrawal. Furthermore, we predict that the emotional intensity in the first 72 hours post-event will be a significant predictor of medium-term distress.
proposals.method: We will recruit 200 Australian adolescents (aged 14-17) with a recent (<48 hour) victimization event, identified via screening of clinical and school settings. Participants will complete a 21-day daily diary survey via a secure mobile app. Daily items will assess emotional states (e.g., shame, anger, anxiety), coping behaviors, and social interactions, adapted from established measures used in Finkelhor's prior work. Data will be analyzed using multilevel growth curve modeling to map trajectories, and time-lagged multilevel vector autoregression (ML-VAR) to examine daily within-person dynamics and temporal precedence between emotions and behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, empirical map of the short-term emotional and behavioral consequences of different victimization types. It will advance the field beyond static correlates to a process-oriented understanding, directly informing the development of just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs). The findings will also clarify the phenomenological overlap and distinctions between sibling aggression and peer victimization.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves decisively from large-scale, retrospective survey methodology to an intensive, prospective micro-longitudinal design focused on the critical post-victimization period. Applying dynamical systems analysis (ML-VAR) to daily diary data in this population is a novel integration of method and subject matter.

proposals.title: Mapping the Enabling Ecosystem: A Latent Class Analysis of Familial and Social Contexts Associated with Child Maltreatment in a National Sample
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on child maltreatment, including Finkelhor's own work on polyvictimization and the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS), has robustly documented victim experiences and individual-level correlates. However, a critical gap exists in systematically identifying and classifying the broader, co-occurring familial and social conditions—the enabling ecosystems—that precede and surround abusive acts. Understanding these contextual constellations is essential for moving beyond reactive intervention to proactive, ecological prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, empirically derived classes of enabling ecosystem characteristics (e.g., combinations of caregiver mental health, substance use, social isolation, economic stress, and intergenerational violence) will be significantly associated with specific types and severities of child maltreatment.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of the ACMS dataset, which provides detailed national survey data on maltreatment and associated items. Using latent class analysis (LCA), a person-centered statistical modeling technique, we will analyze variables describing the familial and social environment preceding maltreatment incidents. The analysis will identify distinct ecosystem profiles. We will then use multinomial logistic regression to test associations between these latent classes and maltreatment types (physical, sexual, emotional abuse, neglect, exposure to domestic violence), controlling for demographic factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first nationally representative typology of pre-abuse ecosystems, shifting the focus from the act/victim to the pathogenic context. It will identify the most common and high-risk combinations of enabling factors, offering a data-driven framework for targeted, multi-dimensional prevention programs that address root systemic causes rather than isolated symptoms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work lists risk factors, this study innovates by modeling how these factors cluster into real-world ecosystem profiles using advanced latent variable modeling on a comprehensive national dataset, moving from a variable-centered to a person- and context-centered understanding of maltreatment etiology.

proposals.title: Identifying Protective Factors Against Child Maltreatment in High-Risk Australian Families: A Secondary Analysis of the Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) Data
proposals.motivation: While decades of research, including Finkelhor's work, have robustly documented the prevalence and risk factors for child maltreatment, a critical gap remains in understanding why some children in high-risk environments do *not* experience abuse. This deficit limits the development of targeted, strengths-based prevention strategies. The recent Australian Child Maltreatment Study (ACMS) provides a unique, nationally representative dataset to systematically investigate these protective factors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, modifiable family, community, and child-level factors (e.g., positive parental mental health, strong non-parental adult support, high child self-regulation) will be significantly associated with the absence of maltreatment among children exposed to established socio-demographic and familial risk factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of the ACMS data. The analysis will first identify a 'high-risk' subsample based on established pre-2026 risk indicators (e.g., parental substance abuse, poverty, parental mental illness). Within this subsample, we will use logistic regression models to test associations between the absence of maltreatment (the outcome variable) and a suite of candidate protective factors measured in the ACMS, such as parental warmth, community cohesion, and child emotional competencies. Analyses will control for demographic variables and the severity of risk exposure.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field from a deficit-focused model to a protection-focused one, providing an empirical map of factors that interrupt maltreatment pathways. The findings will directly inform the design of secondary prevention programs in Australia and internationally by highlighting specific, measurable assets that can be bolstered in vulnerable families.
proposals.novelty: This study applies a resilience lens to a definitive national prevalence dataset, shifting the analytical focus from 'what goes wrong' to 'what goes right' in high-risk contexts. It leverages the ACMS's detailed measurement of both adverse and positive childhood experiences to answer a question its original methodology was uniquely positioned to address but did not centrally analyze.

ideas.title: Mapping the Spectrum of Coercion: A Multi-Axis Behavioral Framework for Non-Force Mechanisms in Child Sexual Abuse
ideas.description: This research moves beyond the binary of force versus no-force to systematically map the specific behavioral tactics used by perpetrators in the absence of overt physical force. It employs a sequential mixed-methods design, beginning with in-depth qualitative analysis of victim/survivor narratives to inductively construct a detailed 'Coercive Tactics Framework' (CTF) categorizing mechanisms like grooming, emotional blackmail, and authority-based compliance. The CTF is then quantitatively validated in a larger sample to assess its reliability and its predictive power for trauma-related outcomes. The final output is a robust, empirically grounded typology that can improve risk assessment, legal understanding, and support services by precisely identifying the pathways of harm.

ideas.title: Micro-Environmental Pathways: Testing Distinct Causal Mechanisms for Different Crimes at the Family and Neighborhood Level
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that different forms of victimization (e.g., child sexual abuse, domestic assault, burglary) declined for distinct reasons, not a single universal cause, by examining how macro-level factors manifest and interact within the same high-risk micro-environments, such as specific families, households, or street blocks. It employs a comparative, multi-method design within these micro-units to trace whether factors like increased guardianship, changing norms, or economic shifts had differential effects on the opportunity structures and perpetrator motivations for each crime type. The synergy allows for a disaggregated, mechanism-based explanation of the crime decline that neither micro-level studies of general risk nor macro-level comparisons of crime types could achieve alone.

ideas.title: Apply Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design (CPTED) and spatial econometrics to analyze child victimization
ideas.description: This idea adapts spatial analysis methods from urban criminology, specifically Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design (CPTED) research and spatial econometrics, to the study of child victimization declines. The analogous solution uses geographically weighted regression (GWR) to analyze crime hotspots, correlating them with place-based environmental and socio-economic factors to understand local drivers. Adapted here, the research would map declines in child victimization and use GWR to test for spatial clustering while modeling how the strength of correlation with factors like community resource access, policing patterns, and economic deprivation varies across different neighborhoods or regions. This reveals not just if declines are clustered, but which local conditions are most associated with improvement in specific areas, enabling more precisely targeted policy and prevention strategies.

ideas.title: Study CSA in exploitative non-force relationships
ideas.description: Focus research on abuse occurring through age difference or caretaking responsibility without overt force.

ideas.description: Map declines in child victimization to test for spatial clustering and correlate with local factor levels.

ideas.title: Cohort-Linked Persistence Analysis: Tracking Non-Declining Victimization Subpopulations Over the Life Course
ideas.description: This research fuses the identification of child victimization subpopulations that resist decline with longitudinal cohort tracking to create a powerful analytical model. It will establish long-term cohorts from these specific, persistent victimization contexts (e.g., certain types of online exploitation, familial neglect in specific demographics) and follow them prospectively. This design allows researchers to directly investigate whether persistence is driven by factors like under-reporting that continues into adulthood, revictimization patterns, or systemic failures in intervention efficacy across the life course. The synergy reveals the mechanisms of persistence by observing their real-time effects on a cohort's trajectory, rather than inferring them from cross-sectional data.

ideas.title: Micro-longitudinal study of victimization aftermath

ideas.title: Geospatial Pharmacoepidemiology of Child Victimization: Mapping the Impact of Psychiatric Medication Access on Maltreatment Hotspots
ideas.description: This research integrates geospatial epidemiology with pharmacoepidemiology to investigate how local rates of appropriate psychiatric medication use in parents and/or children are associated with the geographic distribution and intensity of child maltreatment hotspots. It employs spatial regression and cluster analysis to test whether areas with higher medication utilization show reduced spatial clustering of victimization, after controlling for other community factors. The approach can identify specific regions where improving access to or adherence with specific pharmacological treatments could have the greatest population-level impact on breaking cycles of maltreatment. This moves the field from understanding individual mechanisms to informing targeted, place-based public health interventions that leverage psychiatric care as a community prevention strategy.

ideas.title: Challenge that all contact with age difference is exploitative

ideas.title: Adapting the CONSORT Model to Standardize Child Sexual Abuse Intervention Research
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from clinical medicine, where the CONSORT statement solved the problem of inconsistent and incomplete reporting in randomized controlled trials, this idea proposes to adapt that solution to child sexual abuse research. The analogous field is Clinical Medicine, where the analogous solution was the creation of a mandatory, standardized checklist (CONSORT) to ensure transparent and complete reporting of all trial elements. The adapted idea is to develop a CSA-RR (Child Sexual Abuse Research Reporting) framework, which would provide field-specific, standardized reporting guidelines for different types of CSA research. This would mandate detailed reporting on critical elements such as case definitions, recruitment strategies (especially for hidden populations), ethical protocols, validated measurement instruments, and analytical techniques, thereby dramatically improving the quality, transparency, and comparability of research findings across the field.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Compartment Models to Map Child Victimization Pathways
ideas.description: This idea adapts the system dynamics approach of epidemiological compartment modeling (e.g., the SIR model for disease spread) to the problem of child victimization. The analogous solution from epidemiology involves simulating how populations move between defined health states (Susceptible, Infected, Recovered) based on rates influenced by feedback loops. Adapted to this context, we define key states in the child victimization system (e.g., At-Risk, Experiencing Victimization, In Recovery/Resilient) and model the flows between them. Feedback loops are formalized to simulate how interacting factors—such as parental substance abuse (increasing risk), school-based prevention programs (decreasing risk), and trauma-informed care availability (aiding recovery)—influence these transition rates over time. This model can be used as a virtual testbed to simulate the long-term, system-wide impact of different policy and intervention scenarios on reducing victimization prevalence and promoting resilience.

ideas.title: Estimate Child Sexual Abuse Prevalence Using Capture-Recapture and Multiple Systems Estimation
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from ecology—specifically, wildlife population estimation using capture-recapture methods—to the problem of estimating the true prevalence of child sexual abuse. In ecology, scientists estimate hidden animal populations by taking multiple independent samples, marking individuals, and analyzing the overlap to model the total population. This approach is adapted by treating various administrative and survey datasets on child sexual abuse (e.g., from child protection, law enforcement, healthcare, and victimization surveys) as independent 'captures' or samples. By applying statistical techniques like Multiple Systems Estimation (MSE) to the overlap of cases across these incomplete and biased lists, researchers can produce a more rigorous, evidence-based estimate of the total victim population, including the 'dark figure' of cases unknown to any official system.

ideas.title: A Participatory and Adaptive Methodological Commons for Child Sexual Abuse Research
ideas.description: This initiative establishes a dynamic, living framework for child sexual abuse research methodology, governed by a diverse consortium including researchers, survivors, clinicians, and community advocates. Instead of imposing rigid standards, it creates a 'Methodological Commons' featuring modular guidelines for different research contexts (e.g., prevalence studies, intervention trials, qualitative work) built upon core pillars of ethical engagement and transparency. The system actively funds and integrates innovation cycles, where new methods are proposed, comparatively tested, and adapted, ensuring the field evolves through rigorous pluralism and is fundamentally informed by the expertise of those with lived experience.

ideas.title: Test specific factor interactions for victimization decline
ideas.description: Empirically test how proposed explanatory factors like economic prosperity and social intervention interact to produce declines.
ideas.sourceTitle: Poly-victimization: a neglected component in child victimization.

ideas.title: A Robustness-First, Multi-Method Framework for Evaluating Causal Contributions to Declines in Child Victimization
ideas.description: This research employs a causal inference and robustness-testing framework to move beyond simple statistical weighting of factors. It first formally maps proposed explanations into testable causal diagrams to identify required assumptions and potential confounders. The core analysis then uses an ensemble of complementary methods—including structural equation models, non-parametric machine learning, and simulation of social diffusion processes—to estimate the contribution of each factor under varied, plausible scenarios. The final output is not a single set of weights, but a bounded assessment of each factor's likely influence range and the conditions under which its contribution is robust or highly sensitive to modeling choices and unmeasured variables.

ideas.title: Applying Epidemiological Models of Disease Resurgence to Periods of Rising Child Victimization
ideas.description: This research adapts the core methodological framework from epidemiology used to explain the resurgence of infectious diseases (like measles or whooping cough) to the study of historical periods when child victimization rates were rising. In epidemiology, resurgence is analyzed by modeling the interaction between the pathogen (agent), the host population's susceptibility, and the environmental conditions that enable transmission. The analogous solution is to apply this tripartite model to victimization. The adapted idea is to treat rising victimization as an outbreak to be diagnosed. The research would analyze specific historical periods of increase by collecting data on changes in: 1) The 'agent' (e.g., new forms of exploitation enabled by technologies like the early internet or railroad mobility), 2) The 'host susceptibility' of child populations (e.g., demographic changes, economic deprivation reducing protective resources), and 3) The 'socio-legal environment' (e.g., erosion of child labor laws, weakening of social service agencies, cultural permissiveness). By quantitatively and qualitatively assessing the relative change and interaction of these three dimensions, the study would move beyond monocausal explanations and identify the primary drivers and tipping points for different historical surges in victimization.

ideas.description: Use hierarchical models to estimate localized victimization rates for counties or neighborhoods from national survey data.

ideas.title: Applying Comparative Historical Ecology to Child Victimization Trends
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'multiple evidence base' solution from Historical Ecology to the original seed idea of comparing US decline patterns in child victimization to other nations. In Historical Ecology, researchers reconstruct the decline of species or ecosystems by integrating paleoecological data, historical documents, and contemporary observations to identify multi-causal, interacting drivers across different geographical contexts. Adapted to this research context, the study would treat declining child victimization rates as a complex societal phenomenon. It would systematically compile and triangulate disparate longitudinal data sources—such as national victimization surveys, child protection system records, legislative histories, and socio-economic datasets—across a set of developed countries (e.g., UK, Canada, Australia, Nordic nations). The methodological approach would involve constructing comparable multi-factor timelines to rigorously test whether similar constellations of interacting drivers (e.g., the diffusion of prevention technologies, economic inequality trends, specific legal reforms, or changes in family structure and public awareness) explain the observed patterns of decline, convergence, or divergence across different national contexts, thereby providing a more robust, multi-causal explanation than single-country studies permit.

ideas.title: Use developmental psychopathology frameworks
ideas.description: Apply lifespan developmental models to understand pathways from abuse to adult functioning.

ideas.title: The Architecture of Non-Contact Exploitation in Caretaking Relationships: Mechanisms, Grooming, and Systemic Enablers
ideas.description: This research investigates the specific mechanisms by which caretakers (including parents, guardians, teachers, and coaches) leverage their position of trust, authority, and routine access to exploit children through non-contact means, such as coercing the production of sexual images, exposure to pornography, or sexualized communication. It moves beyond treating non-contact acts as isolated behaviors, instead analyzing them as tools within a broader exploitation dynamic that uses the caretaking relationship itself as the primary instrument of coercion, grooming, and secrecy. The study will map how systemic factors—like institutional neglect, technological access, and social isolation—interact with these relational dynamics to enable and sustain exploitation, ultimately proposing new indicators for prevention and intervention that target the exploitative architecture of the relationship rather than just the discrete abusive acts.

ideas.title: Study exploitation dynamics in caretaking relationships

ideas.title: Participatory Co-Modeling and Empirical Calibration of the Child Victimization System
ideas.description: This research system overcomes the subjectivity of traditional system dynamics by embedding a structured participatory process. Experts and stakeholders collaboratively map the system's causal structure, challenging assumptions and ensuring face validity. The model is then empirically grounded by integrating and statistically analyzing multiple longitudinal data sources to estimate parameters and validate dynamic behaviors. The final output is a transparent, data-informed simulation tool that is continuously refined against real-world outcomes, making it robust for testing intervention scenarios.

ideas.title: Question the self-report methodology's completeness
ideas.description: Test if triangulating self-reports with clinical or administrative data reveals significantly higher abuse rates.
ideas.sourceTitle: The prevalence of elder abuse: a random sample survey.

ideas.title: Cohort-Cross-Domain Longitudinal Analysis of Protective and Promotive Pathways
ideas.description: This research employs longitudinal demographic cohort studies to track individuals over time, but expands the analysis beyond victimization to simultaneously measure concurrent trajectories in positive domains like educational attainment, employment, and health. By analyzing these parallel longitudinal pathways within the same cohorts, the study can identify shared drivers of cross-domain improvement and test causal or protective relationships between domains. This enables a systems-level understanding of whether community interventions create generalized resilience or have domain-specific effects, moving beyond siloed outcome analysis to model holistic life-course development.

ideas.title: Relax the assumption that abuse characteristics are primary drivers
ideas.description: Test if post-disclosure factors like legal system involvement explain more variance than abuse characteristics.
ideas.sourceTitle: Impact of sexual abuse on children: a review and synthesis of recent empirical studies.

ideas.title: Challenge that all declines share same causes
ideas.description: Test if different types of victimization (e.g., sexual abuse vs. larceny) declined for distinct reasons.
ideas.sourceTitle: Poly-victimization: a neglected component in child victimization.

ideas.title: Modeling Coercive Control in Child Sexual Abuse as a Non-Newtonian Social Force
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from rheology, the study of material flow. In rheology, materials like gels or pastes deform not through overt, constant force but under persistent, low-grade stress or through the manipulation of internal bonds (e.g., thixotropy), solved by modeling yield stress and time-dependent structural breakdown. Adapted to the context of child sexual abuse, this approach challenges the assumption that force must be overt and immediately present. The research would model the abusive process using rheological concepts, examining how perpetrators apply persistent, low-grade psychological and social stress—such as grooming, emotional manipulation, and exploitation of authority and trust—that functions like a constant load, gradually breaking down a child's protective boundaries and resistance over time. The goal is to develop a framework for identifying and measuring these 'coercive control' dynamics, including the 'yield points' and 'creep' in relational contexts, to better understand, prevent, and adjudicate cases where overt force is absent but coercion is fundamentally present.

ideas.title: Regime-Aware Explanatory Modeling for Post-2004 Child Victimization Trends
ideas.description: This research first statistically tests for structural breaks in child victimization trend data after 2004 to identify distinct temporal regimes where underlying explanatory relationships may have shifted. For each identified regime, it then applies a dual-model approach: rigorously testing the explanatory power of the original multi-factor model from the pre-2004 analysis, and concurrently developing and testing a new candidate model incorporating factors salient to that specific period (e.g., social media proliferation, smartphone adoption, or changes in victim service infrastructure). This method directly addresses the flaw of temporal model staleness by explicitly testing for and adapting to structural changes in the phenomenon's drivers, providing a more robust and nuanced explanation of post-2004 trends.

ideas.title: Study the dual problem: elder self-neglect
ideas.description: Investigate the prevalence and correlates of self-neglect, rather than neglect perpetrated by a caregiver.
ideas.sourceTitle: The prevalence of elder abuse: a random sample survey.

ideas.title: Analyze causes of the reported case increase
ideas.description: Determine if rising case numbers reflect more abuse or better detection and reporting.
ideas.sourceTitle: Current information on the scope and nature of child sexual abuse.

ideas.title: Comparative Resilience Analysis: Testing a Unified Theory of Child Well-being Change
ideas.description: This research investigates whether the factors explaining concurrent improvements in positive child outcome domains (like educational attainment) are the inverse of the factors explaining the persistent lack of decline in specific child victimization types or subpopulations. By conducting a comparative analysis of 'resilient' positive trends and 'stubborn' negative trends, the study aims to test a unified theory of change in child well-being. It will determine if universal systemic shifts (e.g., policy, economic, cultural) affect all domains symmetrically, or if domain-specific buffers and vulnerabilities create asymmetrical outcomes. This approach moves beyond isolated explanations to model the interconnected architecture of progress and stagnation across the child well-being ecosystem.

ideas.title: Disentangling the Drivers of Crime Declines: A Multi-Trajectory, Multi-Mechanism Framework
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that all crime declines share the same causes by adapting a solution from epidemiology. In public health, the concurrent decline of multiple diseases is rigorously investigated by isolating the distinct effects of specific interventions and social changes on each disease's unique transmission pathways. Adapted to criminology, this research would treat crime types (e.g., sexual abuse, larceny) as having distinct causal 'etiologies.' It would employ a multi-mechanism framework, using methods like multivariate time-series or trajectory analysis on longitudinal data to test whether the declines in different crimes are uniquely correlated with specific hypothesized drivers (e.g., changes in policing, economic factors, technology, or social norms), thereby disentangling the unified narrative of the crime decline.

ideas.title: Causal Mediation and Synthetic Control Analysis for Victimization Decline
ideas.description: This research employs a quasi-experimental design, pairing jurisdictions that experienced significant victimization declines with synthetic control units constructed from similar areas that did not. It first establishes a causal effect for a broad 'decline period' intervention using a difference-in-differences framework. Then, it applies causal mediation analysis to rigorously decompose this total effect, quantifying how much is explained by changes in specific factors like economic indicators and social program spending, both independently and through their interactions. This design isolates the impact of proposed explanations from confounding trends, moving beyond correlation to test causal pathways.

ideas.title: Scale down to micro-analysis of single abusive events
ideas.description: Conduct detailed qualitative studies of individual abusive episodes to identify immediate psychological mechanisms.
ideas.sourceTitle: The traumatic impact of child sexual abuse: a conceptualization.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Cohort Analysis of Professionally Unidentified Cases to Map Systemic Detection Gaps
ideas.description: This research establishes demographic cohorts not from professionally identified victims, but from populations at high risk based on administrative or social determinants data. It then tracks these cohorts longitudinally to observe which individuals eventually become identified by systems (e.g., child protection, healthcare, justice) and, crucially, which remain unidentified over time. By comparing the long-term outcomes, reporting patterns, and life trajectories of the 'ever-identified' versus 'never-identified' sub-cohorts, the study maps the systemic conditions and demographic predictors that perpetuate non-identification. This approach quantifies the scale and nature of the unidentified population dynamically, revealing how detection gaps evolve across the life course and interact with other institutional touchpoints.

ideas.title: Challenge spouse as primary abuser assumption

ideas.title: Decomposing the Decline: A Variance Partitioning Approach to Child Victimization Trends
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the variance partitioning methodology common in community ecology to the problem of explaining broad declines in child victimization. In ecology, this technique is used to disentangle the relative contributions of multiple, correlated environmental drivers on biological communities. The adapted solution would involve building a comprehensive statistical model with time-series or panel data, incorporating all major hypothesized explanatory variables (socioeconomic, policy, technological, cultural). Using hierarchical partitioning or similar techniques, the analysis would quantify the proportion of variance in victimization trends uniquely explained by each factor, as well as the variance explained jointly by combinations of factors. This moves beyond simple correlation to provide a rigorous, quantitative assessment of each factor's relative contribution, identifying primary drivers and interdependent effects within the complex social ecosystem.

ideas.title: Apply confidential survey methods from sensitive topics research

ideas.title: Identifying Resilience Factors in Persistent Child Victimization: An Ecological Niche Analogy
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from ecology and invasive species biology, where the persistence of a species despite broad eradication efforts is explained by identifying protective niche factors. The analogous solution involves mapping the specific environmental conditions (microclimates, resources) that shield subpopulations. Adapted to child victimization research, this means shifting focus from victim or offender subpopulations alone to analyzing the resilient 'niches' that enable certain victimization types to resist decline. The research would systematically identify and model the interacting social, technological, legal, and economic factors that create these protective environments for persistent exploitation, such as specific online platform architectures combined with encryption tools and transnational jurisdictional gaps.

ideas.title: Dual problem: Study non-abusive caretaker relationships
ideas.description: Examine healthy caretaker-child relationships to define the boundaries of exploitative dynamics.

ideas.title: Applying Climate Attribution Methodology to Post-2004 Victimization Trends
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'detection and attribution' methodology from climate science to the study of child victimization trends. In climate science, researchers use models to separate the signal of human-caused forcing from natural variability in long-term data. Analogously, we propose treating the multi-factor model that explained pre-2004 victimization trends as a hypothesized 'social forcing.' The research would apply this model to post-2004 data, using time-series and decomposition techniques to statistically detect whether the previously identified trend pattern persists. The core analytical task is to attribute observed changes after 2004 to either: 1) the continued, diminished, or amplified effect of the original explanatory factors, or 2) new sources of 'variability' not captured by the old model, thereby rigorously testing the persistence and limits of the original framework's explanatory power.

ideas.title: Pharmaco-Criminological Network Analysis of Maltreatment Risk and Resilience
ideas.description: This research applies criminal network analysis (CNA) methods to map the social and familial networks where child maltreatment occurs, but integrates pharmacological data as a key variable. It investigates how a parent's or child's adherence to specific psychiatric medications (e.g., for depression, impulsivity, or substance use) changes their position, influence, and risk within the network over time. The synergy allows for modeling medication effect as a network-disruption mechanism, predicting how treatment in key nodes can prevent cascade victimization and improve overall network resilience, moving beyond static profiling to dynamic intervention modeling.

ideas.title: Modeling the Social Pathogens: An Epidemiological Framework for Abuse-Enabling Conditions
ideas.description: This research applies compartmental models from epidemiology to map the propagation of social and institutional conditions that create environments where abuse is more likely to occur and persist. Instead of modeling abuse transmission, it treats factors like organizational secrecy, normalized harmful behaviors, and ineffective oversight as 'contagions' spreading through networks. The system integrates anonymized data on institutional policies, whistleblower outcomes, and cultural surveys to identify high-risk clusters and critical intervention points for strengthening protective social structures.

ideas.title: Relax assumption that all exposures are harmful

ideas.title: Modeling the Epidemic of Neglect: How Economic Prosperity Alters the Contagion Dynamics of Child Maltreatment
ideas.description: This research applies epidemiological compartmental and network models (SIR, etc.) to child maltreatment data, specifically testing the hypothesis that economic growth non-uniformly alters risk landscapes. It posits that while prosperity may reduce 'contact-based' harms like violent crime (akin to reducing transmission rates), it may simultaneously increase susceptibility to 'environmental' harms like neglect by altering family structures, social isolation, and time pressures—factors modeled as changes in population susceptibility and clustering. The synergy allows for simulating counterfactual scenarios to project how different growth patterns (e.g., unequal, rapid) might shift the endemic equilibrium and spatial clustering of maltreatment subtypes, offering a dynamic systems view of prosperity's dual role as both a preventive and a risk factor.

ideas.title: Telephone surveys adequately capture sensitive victimization
