ideas.title: Cohort-Specific Decomposition of Victimization Life-Cycles
ideas.description: This research applies time-series decomposition techniques to longitudinal data from specific demographic victim cohorts. By separating the long-term trend, cyclical patterns, and residual noise within each cohort's victimization and reporting data over time, the analysis can determine if observed outcomes are consistent across all groups or are cohort-specific. This allows for testing whether explanations for patterns (e.g., declining reporting) are attributable to broad societal shifts, lifecycle stages, or the unique experiences of a particular generation. The synergy creates a powerful method to move beyond describing aggregate trends to explaining their differential origins across population subgroups.

ideas.title: Decomposing the Social Signal: Isolating the Causal Impact of Normative Reversals on Victimization Trends
ideas.description: This research applies time-series decomposition techniques from signal processing (e.g., STL, X-13ARIMA-SEATS) to long-term victimization and social indicator data. The goal is to isolate the cyclical and trend components potentially attributable to the post-1960s normative reversal (e.g., shifts in communal trust, institutional legitimacy, or collective efficacy) from other long-term secular trends (like economic cycles or demographic shifts) and short-term noise. By correlating the timing, phase, and amplitude of these isolated 'normative reversal' signal components with changes in victimization rates, the study aims to provide rigorous, quantitative evidence for whether the dissipation of 1960s-era social changes had a masked negative effect on societal well-being, distinct from other concurrent positive factors.

ideas.title: Test for shared causal architecture across positive societal trends
ideas.description: This research empirically tests whether the interactive combinations of factors (e.g., economic prosperity * social intervention) that explain declines in victimization are the same as those explaining concurrent improvements in other positive domains like educational attainment or public health. By applying the same interaction-testing framework across multiple outcome domains, it can identify if there is a shared 'causal architecture' for societal progress or if different domains are driven by unique factor synergies. This allows for a stronger test of general theories of social improvement and can inform whether policy portfolios should be integrated or tailored for specific outcomes.

ideas.title: Modeling Pharmacological Cascade Effects on Family System Dynamics to Prevent Maltreatment
ideas.description: This research idea uses an analogy from ecosystem ecology, where solutions involve modeling cascading effects of targeted interventions through complex systems. The analogous solution is the study of how reintroducing a keystone predator creates trophic cascades that restore ecosystem stability, solved via dynamic modeling of direct and indirect pathways. Adapted to psychiatric pharmacology, the idea is to investigate how medication use in parents or children reduces maltreatment by modeling the family as a dynamic system. The study would map the pharmacological cascade: tracing how symptom alleviation (the direct intervention) propagates through behavioral, emotional, and relational pathways (e.g., improved parental regulation, reduced child aggression, enhanced parent-child synchrony) to ultimately prevent maltreatment and associated victimization. This approach moves beyond correlating medication with outcomes to explicitly test and quantify the mediating mechanisms within the family system.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Deconstruction of Persistence: Generating and Testing Causal Hypotheses for Stagnant Child Victimization
ideas.description: This research program first identifies child victimization types or subpopulations showing statistical resistance to aggregate decline. Its core innovation is a structured adversarial process to generate competing, testable causal hypotheses for this persistence, such as technological affordances, institutional fragmentation, or resilient offender networks. The final phase designs and proposes specific empirical tests—leveraging policy variations, technological shifts, or network analyses—to falsify or support these mechanistic explanations, moving beyond description to actionable causal understanding.

ideas.title: Dynamic Cohort Retention with Attrition-Corrected Longitudinal Analysis
ideas.description: This system establishes victim cohorts with a pre-plined, multi-contact protocol designed to minimize and account for participant attrition over time. It employs a mix of active follow-up methods and passive data linkages (where ethically permissible) to maintain engagement, especially with high-risk, mobile, or disengaging subgroups. All longitudinal data collection is paired with a concurrent statistical analysis plan that explicitly models the mechanisms of dropout and applies correction techniques to produce less biased estimates of long-term outcomes. The result is a more robust and representative understanding of victim trajectories and reporting patterns over extended periods.

ideas.title: System Dynamics Modeling of Non-Contact Child Sexual Exploitation: Feedback Loops and Digital Ecosystem Evolution
ideas.description: This research applies system dynamics modeling to map and simulate the complex causal structure of non-contact sexual exploitation of children (e.g., online grooming, sextortion, live-streamed abuse). It explicitly models feedback loops, such as how offender anonymity fuels experimentation, which expands victim pools and normalizes behaviors, thereby recruiting more offenders. The model integrates distinct digital ecosystem factors—like platform design, cryptocurrency flows, and content dissemination networks—as dynamic stocks and flows. This allows for testing how interventions in the digital environment (e.g., changes to platform architecture or cryptocurrency regulation) might dampen these feedback loops over time, shifting the focus from reactive victim rescue to proactive system disruption.

ideas.title: Applying Factorial Experimental Design from Ecology to Test Factor Interactions in Victimization Decline
ideas.description: This idea adapts the factorial experimental design methodology from community ecology to the problem of testing interactions between explanatory factors for victimization decline. In ecology, such designs are used to disentangle the combined effects of multiple drivers on an outcome like biodiversity. The analogous solution involves controlled manipulation and combination of factors to measure interaction effects. Adapted to criminology, the research would employ a quasi-experimental, longitudinal design, treating cities or matched neighborhoods as units of analysis. By constructing statistical models with interaction terms for factors like economic prosperity and social intervention, and by carefully creating comparison groups through matching techniques, the study can empirically test the specific nature of their interactions—whether they operate independently, reinforce each other (synergy), or dampen each other's effects—in producing declines in victimization.

ideas.title: Agent-Based Epidemiological Modeling of Abuse Dynamics
ideas.description: This research synthesizes agent-based modeling (ABM) from complexity science with formal epidemiological frameworks (e.g., SIR, SEIR) to model the social transmission of abuse. Heterogeneous agents (individuals, families, institutions) interact within simulated social networks, with rules for 'exposure,' 'transmission,' and 'recovery' informed by sociological and psychological risk factors. This allows for the bottom-up emergence of macro-level abuse trends and clustering, enabling the testing of how specific interventions (e.g., community support programs, policy changes) might micro-found observed declines by altering the transmission pathways within the simulated system.

ideas.title: Multi-Level Time-Series Decomposition: Linking Macro Trends to Micro-Level Victimization Mechanisms
ideas.description: This approach first decomposes aggregate victimization time series into trend, cyclical, and irregular components using signal processing methods. These components are then treated as contextual, time-varying covariates within a Bayesian hierarchical model that incorporates individual or neighborhood-level panel data. The system explicitly tests for cross-level interactions, assessing whether the macro-level trends and cycles moderate known individual risk factors or represent emergent population-level phenomena. This design prevents ecological fallacy by directly modeling the linkage between temporal patterns and the heterogeneous processes that generate victimization events.

ideas.title: Agent-Based Modeling of Competing Social Outcomes: Micro-Founding Divergent Trends in Victimization and Attainment
ideas.description: This research uses agent-based modeling (ABM) to build a synthetic society of individuals and families whose behaviors and outcomes are shaped by a common set of theorized macro-factors (e.g., economic shifts, policy changes, technological adoption). The key innovation is to simulate multiple outcome domains—such as risk of victimization and progress in educational attainment—concurrently within the same model run. This allows for testing if a single, unified set of micro-level rules can generate the empirically observed divergent national trends, or if competing theoretical explanations are required, thereby uncovering the complex interdependencies between social problems and positive developments.

ideas.title: Applying Common Cause Analysis from Ecology to Positive Outcome Scaling
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from community ecology, where researchers disentangle whether correlated population changes across species are due to a shared environmental driver or independent niche factors. The analogous solution uses structural equation modeling to test common cause versus independent cause models. Adapted to scaling research in social science, the research would formally test if concurrent improvements in non-victimization domains (e.g., educational attainment, mental health, social cohesion) and victimization reduction are best explained by a latent shared factor (e.g., increased collective efficacy, general well-being) or by distinct, domain-specific pathways of an intervention. This moves beyond simple correlation to model the causal structure linking intervention components to a suite of positive outcomes.

ideas.title: Apply Network Immunization Strategies from Computer Virology to Abuse Prevention
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from computer network security and virology to the problem of understanding and preventing abuse. The analogous field uses epidemiological models on contact networks to simulate malware spread and then applies network immunization algorithms (e.g., targeting nodes by degree, betweenness, or other centrality measures) to contain outbreaks. The adapted idea applies this approach to model the spread of abusive behaviors or norms within a social or institutional network. By identifying the network's most critical nodes—such as influential individuals, hubs of activity, or bridging connections between clusters—prevention efforts can be strategically targeted to 'immunize' these points, thereby disrupting the most probable transmission pathways and maximizing the impact of limited intervention resources on reducing overall abuse prevalence.

ideas.title: Disaggregating the Prosperity-Risk Nexus: A Subnational, Causal-Mechanism Test of Growth's Divergent Effects on Social Pathologies
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a simple uniform assessment to investigate how economic growth differentially impacts social risks across populations and places. Using decades of subnational data, it tests specific causal mechanisms linking prosperity to changes in both 'neglect-type' (e.g., child welfare cases, elder isolation) and 'crime-type' (e.g., violent, property) outcomes. The analysis identifies the contextual factors—such as inequality levels, social spending, and demographic composition—that determine whether prosperity in a given locality reduces certain risks while exacerbating others. The result is a contingent, evidence-based model of prosperity's multifaceted social consequences.

ideas.title: Bias-Audited Dynamic Network Analysis for Proactive Exploitation Risk Intervention
ideas.description: This research develops a computational framework that models the dynamic evolution of networks associated with high-risk environments for exploitation (e.g., fraud, human trafficking). Instead of static offender profiling, it analyzes the flux of relationships, resources, and vulnerabilities to identify structural precursors to criminal activity. The system incorporates continuous adversarial auditing to identify and mitigate biases inherited from law enforcement data, ensuring risk flags are tied to behaviors and network positions rather than demographics. The output is designed to guide timely, resource-based interventions—such as offering support to potential victims or disrupting financial pipelines—shifting the focus from post-crime apprehension to pre-crime harm reduction.

ideas.title: Applying Epidemiological Contact Tracing and Compartmental Models to Criminal Network Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts core epidemiological methods to the criminological context of profiling offender networks. The analogous field is Epidemiology, where contact tracing is used to map infection pathways and compartmental models (e.g., SIR models) are employed to understand disease spread dynamics, predict outbreaks, and identify super-spreaders. The adapted research idea applies this framework by treating criminal networks as analogous to contagion networks. It involves using modified contact tracing to map relationships and the 'transmission' of criminal behavior or influence. Furthermore, it adapts compartmental models to categorize individuals within a social network into states such as 'Susceptible' (to criminal influence), 'Active' (engaged in offending), and 'Removed' (no longer active due to intervention). This approach aims to quantitatively model the dynamics of criminal network expansion, identify pivotal 'super-spreader' offenders who recruit or influence many others, and simulate the potential spread of criminal activity to inform targeted disruption strategies.

ideas.title: Apply Empirical Mode Decomposition from Geophysics to Isolate Non-Stationary Cycles in Victimization Data
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD) method from geophysics to the analysis of victimization data. In geophysics, EMD is used to analyze complex, non-stationary signals like seismic waves by adaptively decomposing them into intrinsic mode functions (IMFs) and a residual trend. Applying this to crime or victimization time-series data would allow researchers to separate the signal into data-driven cyclical components, a long-term trend, and noise without assuming linearity or stationarity. The resulting IMFs can then be rigorously tested against proposed social, economic, and policy explanations (e.g., policing cycles, unemployment fluctuations, legislative impacts) for a more nuanced and empirically grounded understanding of the drivers behind changes in victimization rates.

ideas.title: Measuring the Socioeconomic Impact of Post-1970s Policy Reversals from 1960s-Era Social Reform Trajectories
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that specific policy and normative reversals from trajectories established in the 1960s (e.g., shifts toward mass incarceration, welfare restriction, or erosion of gender equity frameworks) generated significant negative socioeconomic outcomes, which were subsequently obscured by broader macroeconomic or technological gains. It identifies discrete reversal events within defined policy domains and employs a mixed-methods counterfactual analysis, comparing outcomes in jurisdictions or demographic groups most affected by the reversals against plausible control scenarios. The goal is to isolate the causal impact of these reversals from other positive late-20th century trends to assess their net societal effect.

ideas.title: Disentangling System-Driven Detection from True Prevalence Trends in Case Identification
ideas.description: This research aims to rigorously separate the contribution of systemic and diagnostic factors from changes in underlying population prevalence when analyzing trends in identified cases. It will employ a quasi-experimental design, utilizing interrupted time-series analysis around policy changes, comparisons to external validation cohorts with stable ascertainment, and counterfactual models to estimate the proportion of the observed increase attributable to improved detection versus true rise. The outcome will be a quantified attribution of the case increase trend to specific system-level drivers and estimated true prevalence changes, providing a robust evidence base for public health planning.

ideas.title: A Configurational and Mechanistic Framework for Cross-Domain Positive Outcome Analysis
ideas.description: This research moves beyond testing if the 'same factors' explain improvements across domains by first mapping the distinct causal recipes and contextual mechanisms leading to success in each domain (e.g., education, health, safety). Using a mixed-methods design, it identifies necessary conditions and sufficient configurations of factors for each outcome through within-case analysis and Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA). It then employs a coordinated statistical modeling strategy to test the portability and interaction of these domain-specific configurations, explicitly accounting for equifinality and cross-domain spillovers to build a robust, mechanistic theory of concurrent community improvement.

ideas.title: Joint Latent-State Modeling of True Incidence and Reporting Artifacts Using Multiple Data Streams
ideas.description: This research develops a unified modeling framework that simultaneously estimates true disease prevalence and time-varying reporting probabilities. It integrates disparate data sources, such as case reports, seroprevalence studies, wastewater concentrations, and syndromic surveillance, which have different functional relationships to the underlying latent state of true incidence. By formally modeling the measurement processes for each stream, the system can statistically identify whether observed declines are more consistent with changes in transmission or changes in reporting behavior, moving beyond narrative speculation to quantitative inference.

ideas.title: Adapting Dark Matter Detection Methods to Model Reporting Bias in Epidemiological Data
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that reported data directly reflects true prevalence by drawing an analogy from astrophysics. In dark matter searches, raw detector data is not equated with true particle flux; instead, complex models account for detector efficiency, background noise, and environmental factors to infer the underlying signal. Adapting this solution, we propose to model the public health reporting system as an imperfect detector. The research would develop a mathematical framework where the observed decline in reported cases is an output generated by the convolution of two latent processes: the true biological incidence and a time-varying 'reporting behavior function.' This function would include parameters for factors like testing rates, diagnostic thresholds, healthcare-seeking behavior, and surveillance intensity. By using Bayesian inference or similar methods to invert this model, one could jointly estimate the most likely trajectories for both true prevalence and reporting probability, potentially revealing that apparent declines are partly or wholly artifacts of changing detection rather than true epidemiological change.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Risk Trade-off Models to Economic Prosperity
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that economic prosperity has uniformly positive effects on societal well-being by proposing that it may create a trade-off between different categories of risk, analogous to risk trade-offs in ecosystem ecology. Drawing from the Intermediate Disturbance Hypothesis and ecological stability-resilience models, where high stability can reduce biodiversity and increase vulnerability to novel shocks, we adapt this framework to socio-economics. The adapted idea investigates economic growth not as a linear reducer of all risks, but as a transformative force that suppresses acute, direct risks (e.g., violent crime due to material deprivation) while potentially amplifying chronic, indirect, or novel risks (e.g., institutional neglect, social isolation, complex systemic failures). The research would involve developing a multi-dimensional risk model to quantify how a society's 'risk portfolio' shifts with prosperity, identifying specific prosperity-amplified risks and the mechanisms behind these trade-offs.

ideas.title: Causally-Informed Factor Analysis (CIFA): Using Quasi-Experimental Variation to Identify Structural Latent Variable Models
ideas.description: This approach first identifies exogenous shocks or instrumental variables for observed, manipulable variables that are theorized to be indicators or causes of latent factors. These causal estimates are then used as constraints within a structural equation modeling (SEM) framework to estimate the latent factor model. The result is a factor model where the latent constructs and their relationships to observed variables are identified by quasi-experimental variation, moving beyond mere correlation. This produces factor scores and loadings that can support stronger causal claims about the role of underlying constructs in subsequent analyses.

ideas.title: Agent-Based Modeling of Social Intervention Decay: Uncovering Systemic Drivers of Declining Reach and Effectiveness
ideas.description: This research integrates the counter-intuitive hypothesis that social interventions can decay with the methodological power of agent-based modeling (ABM). It constructs a computational simulation populated by heterogeneous agents (individuals, families, service providers) whose rules of interaction, adaptation, and resource access are informed by real-world data. The model is used to run experiments, manipulating macro-factors like policy changes, economic shocks, or social network fragmentation to observe how they generate emergent declines in intervention reach and effectiveness at the population level. This allows for the discovery of hidden mechanisms and tipping points that drive decay, moving beyond correlation to a theory of causal process.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Models to Map the Decline and Replacement of Social Interventions
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that social interventions always increase in reach or effectiveness by drawing an analogy from ecology, specifically the study of ecological succession. The analogous solution involves modeling how biological communities change, where dominant species can decline and be replaced. Adapted to social research, this means examining social interventions not as static or perpetually expanding entities, but as dynamic programs within a competitive 'ecosystem.' The study would map the temporal trajectory of specific interventions to identify phases of decline, plateau, or replacement, analyzing the contextual factors—such as shifting political priorities, funding cycles, emerging evidence, or public discourse—that act as 'environmental pressures' leading to decreased reach or effectiveness, thereby providing a more nuanced understanding of intervention lifecycles.

ideas.title: Multi-Site Micro-Environment Analysis of Macro-Micro Mechanism Transfer
ideas.description: This research tests how macro-level factors manifest by implementing a coordinated study of replicated high-risk micro-environments across diverse macro-contexts. Using a standardized protocol of embedded sensors, longitudinal surveys, and ethnographic observation, it collects comparable deep data within households or neighborhoods in multiple cities or regions. The core analysis then performs a structured cross-case comparison to isolate which observed micro-dynamics are universal mechanisms linking macro factors to local outcomes, and which are contingent on specific macro-context, thereby producing generalizable, mechanistic insights rather than isolated case studies.

ideas.title: Apply network analysis to sleep components
ideas.description: Use network analysis methods to model interactions between different sleep quality components and pregnancy outcomes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sexual abuse in a national survey of adult men and women: prevalence, characteristics, and risk factors.

ideas.title: Causal decomposition of the professional identification gap using multi-source latent class models
ideas.description: This research moves beyond documenting missed cases to formally modeling the causes of the identification gap. It employs latent class analysis on integrated data from professionals, alternative screening tools, and outcome registries to estimate a latent 'true status' as a reference. The model then quantifies how much of the gap is attributable to systemic factors (e.g., access, guidelines), cognitive biases, or inherent case ambiguity. The output is a diagnostic framework that identifies specific, addressable leverage points for improving professional identification systems, transforming a descriptive critique into an engineering tool.

ideas.title: A Mechanism-First Framework for Modeling Exploitation in Asymmetric Relationships
ideas.description: This research develops a comparative model of exploitation by first identifying core transactional mechanisms (e.g., control over essential resources, strategic dependency creation, normative weaponization) independent of any specific relationship context. It then applies this model to analyze how these mechanisms are operationalized, facilitated, or mitigated across different asymmetric relational structures, including caretaking scenarios, age-disparate partnerships, and mentor-protégé dynamics. The design includes control cases of equitable asymmetric relationships to distinguish exploitative processes from the asymmetry itself. The outcome is a formal taxonomy of exploitation mechanisms and their contextual amplifiers, enabling more precise intervention.

ideas.sourceTitle: Sexual abuse in a national survey of adult men and women: prevalence, characteristics, and risk factors.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Trophic Cascade Analysis to the Reversal of 1960s Social Norms
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a classic problem in ecology—understanding the full, often counterintuitive impact of reintroducing a keystone species—to the challenge of assessing the negative consequences of reversing 1960s social norms. In ecology, the solution involves introducing a change (a predator) and then mapping the resulting trophic cascade through the entire food web, using controlled observation and modeling to separate its effects from other variables. Adapted here, the research would select a specific, well-defined reversal of a 1960s norm (the 'keystone change') and meticulously trace its hypothesized 'cascade' through interconnected social, economic, and political systems over subsequent decades. The methodological core is to adapt ecological network analysis and counterfactual modeling to isolate the impacts of this normative reversal from other concurrent positive factors (e.g., GDP growth, digital revolution), aiming to reveal masked negative consequences in areas like social cohesion, mental health, or economic inequality.

ideas.title: One-year recall period is optimal for measurement
ideas.description: Compare prevalence estimates using different recall periods to identify bias from memory decay or telescoping.
ideas.sourceTitle: Violence, Abuse, and Crime Exposure in a National Sample of Children and Youth

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Compartmental Modeling for Agent-Based Social Simulation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from epidemiology to the social science context of the original agent-based modeling seed. In epidemiology, compartmental models (like SIR) solve the problem of linking individual-level disease transmission to population-level epidemic dynamics by defining clear agent states and probabilistic transition rules between them based on contact. We adapt this by defining analogous social or economic states for family agents (e.g., 'thriving', 'struggling', 'exiting a demographic trend') and creating transition rules driven by interactions with simulated macro factors (e.g., housing cost inflation, access to childcare). This adapted agent-based model will micro-found observed macro-level social declines by simulating how these 'states' spread or recede through a virtual population, revealing the nonlinear dynamics and tipping points analogous to epidemic spread or containment.

ideas.title: Adapting Dark Matter Detection to Professional Identification Gaps
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that professional identification should be the primary metric for measuring a phenomenon. Drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where 'dark matter' constitutes most of the universe's mass but evades direct detection, the project investigates the vast number of cases not identified by professionals. The analogous solution from cosmology involves inferring the properties of unseen matter through its gravitational effects on visible structures. Adapted to this context, the research would develop methodological tools to infer the scale and nature of unidentified cases by analyzing their indirect signatures—such as systemic anomalies, unexplained resource drains, gaps in service provision, or patterns in secondary data (e.g., administrative records, community surveys, digital traces). The goal is to shift from a reliance on direct professional observation to a model-based, inferential framework that quantifies and characterizes the 'dark population' missing from professional metrics, thereby providing a more complete and accurate understanding of the phenomenon's true scope.

ideas.title: Apply econometric causal inference to factor analysis
ideas.description: Use difference-in-differences or instrumental variable methods to isolate causal effects of specific factors.

ideas.title: Standardize definitions and research methodologies

ideas.title: Modeling Exploitation in Caretaking as a Parasitic Symbiosis
ideas.description: This research seed applies analogical reasoning from evolutionary ecology's study of parasitic symbiosis to the problem of exploitation in caretaking relationships. In ecology, parasitic relationships are analyzed by modeling the cost-benefit exchange between organisms, identifying specific mechanisms like resource siphoning and behavioral manipulation that define the exploitation. Adapting this solution, the research would model caretaking relationships as symbiotic systems to pinpoint and quantify the precise mechanisms of exploitation (e.g., asymmetric resource transfer, dependency manipulation, evasion of safeguards). This framework aims to move beyond descriptive accounts, enabling the dynamic modeling of how these relationships become exploitative, identifying critical tipping points, and suggesting targeted interventions based on disrupting the 'parasitic' mechanisms.

ideas.title: Empirically-Grounded and Calibrated Agent-Based Modeling for Demographic Decline
ideas.description: This research integrates agent-based modeling with robust empirical econometrics and calibration techniques to overcome the 'black box' problem. First, it uses longitudinal micro-data to statistically estimate the key behavioral parameters (e.g., fertility preferences, partnership formation, educational investment decisions) that will govern agent rules, grounding assumptions in observed data. The simulation is then calibrated using pattern-oriented modeling, where its output is iteratively refined until it can simultaneously reproduce a suite of independent, real-world macro patterns (e.g., cohort fertility trends, marriage rate curves, educational attainment distributions). This creates a validated, empirically credible virtual laboratory for testing how micro-level behaviors, interacting under different policy or economic scenarios, generate the observed macro-level declines.

ideas.title: Challenge that increased reporting always indicates success
ideas.description: Examine if rising case numbers could partially reflect system overreach or misidentification.
ideas.sourceTitle: Current information on the scope and nature of child sexual abuse.

ideas.title: Micro-Environmental Stress Testing via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Ecology and Conservation Biology, where scientists model species vulnerability by projecting global climate changes onto local 'ecological niches'. The analogous solution involves downscaling macro-models to specific habitat conditions to predict extinction risk. Adapted to the original social science context, the research would define a 'socio-ecological niche' for a family or neighborhood. By instrumenting these high-risk micro-environments, the study would observe in real-time how macro-factor perturbations (e.g., a policy change, an economic shock, an extreme weather event) manifest and interact locally, effectively stress-testing the micro-system to understand its specific vulnerabilities and resilience mechanisms.

ideas.title: Decomposing the Diagnostic Signal: A Multi-Factor Attribution Framework for Increased Case Identification
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from astronomy/astrophysics, where scientists routinely decompose surges in object detection into multiple causal factors. The analogous problem is attributing a large increase in detected celestial objects to specific drivers like better telescopes, wider surveys, new algorithms, and increased observer focus. The adapted idea investigates the factors driving the large increase in professionally identified cases by developing a multi-factor attribution framework. This framework would quantitatively separate the increase into components stemming from improved diagnostic tools, expanded screening coverage, evolved professional methodologies, and shifts in institutional prioritization, thereby moving beyond a monolithic explanation to a nuanced understanding of each driver's contribution over time.

ideas.title: Apply Causal Deconvolution from Neuroscience to Factor Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts causal deconvolution methods from systems neuroscience to the problem of causal inference in factor analysis. In neuroimaging, techniques like instrumental variable regression are used with randomized stimuli to isolate the causal contribution of a specific cognitive process to the observed, mixed BOLD signal. Similarly, in econometric factor analysis, we can treat an observed variable as a composite measure influenced by multiple latent factors. By identifying a quasi-experimental setting or an external instrument (e.g., a policy change affecting only one underlying economic factor), we can apply difference-in-differences or instrumental variable methods to the estimation of the factor model. This allows us to move beyond identifying correlated latent structures and instead estimate the causal effect of a specific latent factor on the observed variables, thereby performing 'causal factor deconvolution'.

ideas.title: Systematic Archaeology of Social Intervention Dissolution: Tracing Administrative Decay and Participant Diversion
ideas.description: This research systematically identifies social interventions that may have decreased or dissolved entirely, overcoming selection bias by first mining diverse historical records to create a candidate registry. It then employs a mixed-methods 'trace evidence' approach, analyzing administrative decay (e.g., budget line disappearance, staff reassignment, website archiving) and participant flow diversion to other services as primary indicators of decreased reach. The final analysis contextualizes these dissolution pathways within shifting political and funding landscapes, providing a robust account of why and how interventions contract or vanish, not just if they did.
